December 2001|ASHRAE Journal
A Steam Trap Primer
About the Author
S
Roy Ahlgren is director of Bell & Gossett’s “Little
Red Schoolhouse” in Morton Grove, Ill.
By Roy Ahlgren
Associate Member ASHRAE
team traps are special valves used throughout steam systems. They
are installed in steam distribution piping, and on every steam heat
exchanger. Every steam trap must do three important jobs to allow the
steam system to operate at minimum cost: 1) it must be open in order
to drain condensate; 2) it must close to prevent loss of steam; 3) it
must open to vent air and other non-condensable gasses.
Why Do You Need Steam Traps?
Each of these jobs is vital to efficient
system operation.
1. Condensate, which forms as steam
gives up heat to the process, must be re-
moved in order to let the process con-
tinue at the designed rate of heat trans-
fer. If it is not removed, the heat exchanger
will fill with condensate and the heat
transfer rate will fall.
Each pound (0.45 kg) of condensate
will supply only one Btu (1.5 kJ) as it
cools one degree in temperature (0.55 K),
in contrast to almost 1,000 Btus (1055
kJ) transferred to the process as a pound
(0.45 kg) of steam condenses. Conden-
sate will damage the heat exchanger if it
is not drained promptly.
Some damage occurs very rapidly, and
is accompanied by obvious noise and vi-
bration. The loud banging often heard in
steam systems is a result of steam hammer
caused by the collapse of steam bubbles
in retained condensate. Steam hammer can
quickly cause failure of heat exchanger
tubes. Other damage related to conden-
sate retention is less obvious as it occurs.
Several types of corrosion can occur if
condensate is not drained from a heat
exchanger. Some, like generalized cor-
rosion, occurs slowly over a long period
of time. Condensate acts as an electro-
lyte to complete the corrosion process,
thinning the heat exchanger surfaces
until they fail catastrophically. Other cor-
rosion processes, like oxygen pitting and
condensate grooving, concentrate on a
very small surface of the tube and cause
a less spectacular failure in a shorter pe-
riod of time. Clearly condensate must be
removed to allow the process to continue,
and prevent costly damage.
2. If a steam trap fails to prevent steam
leakage from the heat exchanger, the pro-
cess is simply “not getting its money’s
worth” from that steam. The whole point
in building the steam system is to trans-
fer heat in the heat exchanger. Steam leak-
age from a trap also will increase pres-
sure in the condensate piping system,
thus reducing the pressure differential
across other traps in the system.
Since all traps require a pressure dif-
ferential to work properly, they will not
work as designed, either. If steam leak-
age is great enough, it can raise conden-
sate temperatures to the point that cavi-
tation occurs in the condensate pump.
3. It’s important to vent air and other
non-condensable gasses from the system
on start-up, since steam cannot fill the
system until these gasses are vented.
Heating systems that cycle on/off must
push a lot of air out of the system to fill it
with steam. Industrial systems that rarely
shut down only need to vent a little air.
Traps that have failed closed often ac-
count for failure to heat parts of the sys-
tem. Non-condensables accumulating in
a heat exchanger will act as insulators,
reducing the rate of heat transfer. They
also can dissolve in retained condensate,
accelerating the corrosion processes de-
scribed earlier.
The most important “non-condens-
able” is carbon dioxide, a gas that some-
times evolves in the softening process
used to prevent the formation of boiler
scale. Carbon dioxide can dissolve in
cool condensate, lowering the pH of the
water, accelerating condensate grooving
and generalized corrosion.
All traps do the same three jobs, but
they use different properties of steam and
condensate to open and close. All steam
traps require a difference in fluid head
from the trap inlet to the outlet in order
to work. A combination of higher steam
pressure versus lower condensate pres-
sure and higher elevation of the heat ex-
changer with respect to the condensate
What They Are, What They Do
Steam
ASHRAE Journal|December 2001
37
pipe provide that differential head.
•
Thermostatic traps work by recognizing the difference
between hot steam and condensate that has been allowed to
subcool after it formed.
•
Mechanical traps recognize the difference in density be-
tween steam and condensate. They usually use a float.
•
Thermodynamic traps use the difference between high-
velocity, low-density steam and low-velocity, high-density con-
densate to accomplish all three jobs.
Thermostatic Traps
Thermostatic traps use a temperature-sensitive element to
distinguish between steam and subcooled condensate (Figure
1). The element is usually a bellows filled under vacuum with
a small amount of alcohol and water. Sometimes the thermal
element is a capsule full of wax that changes volume with
temperature. Steam is hot enough to cause the alcohol-water
mixture to boil, expand, and close the trap. After the steam
condenses and subcools, the trap opens. Condensate and air
will flow through the trap as long as the pressure upstream is
greater than the pressure downstream.
It’s important to stress that the condensate must subcool
before this thermal element can open the trap. Condensate
forms at the same temperature as the steam. If the steam was hot
enough to close the trap, the condensate is still hot enough to
keep it closed. For subcooling to occur, traps like this must
have a “cooling leg” upstream of the trap. The amount of
subcooling depends on the nature of the thermal element. For
bellows traps, the condensate must cool 15°F–35°F (8°C–19°C)
below steam temperature. In solid-filled traps, the condensate
must cool to about 180°F (82°C) before the trap will open.
Mechanical Traps
The float & thermostatic (F&T) trap and the inverted bucket
trap are common mechanical traps (Figure 2). Each uses a float
attached to a linkage that opens or closes the trap orifice. The
F&T trap float rises and falls with condensate level. It’s a “modu-
lating” trap that can be wide open when condensing rates are
high or partly closed when the rate of condensate formation
drops off. The orifice is low, so condensate always seals it to
prevent steam loss. Air must be vented through a separate vent
element near the top. This vent element is often identical to
the thermal element in the thermostatic trap. The float and the
vent elements work independently to accomplish all three jobs
in the F&T trap.
An inverted bucket trap uses a cup that acts like a float to
close the trap when it fills with steam and becomes buoyant.
When condensate fills the cup, it acts like a weight to pull the
linkage down and open the trap. Inverted bucket traps cycle
fully open or closed, and they must always be “primed,” or
filled with condensate, to close.
Because the orifice is at the top, it’s less likely to clog com-
pared to the F&T trap. Air venting is accomplished through a
small hole in the top of the bucket. Steam can also pass through
this vent, so it’s kept very small. Because of this, these traps are
used only in applications where there isn’t a lot of air venting
required. Neither of these mechanical traps depends on
subcooling. They discharge condensate at close to steam tem-
perature.
Thermodynamic Traps
Thermodynamic traps often use a highly polished disc on
a flat seat as shown in Figure 3. Cool condensate simply rises
through the central port, lifts the disc, and is discharged
through the ring to the trap outlet. As the condensate tem-
perature rises, it will flash as it passes from the higher pres-
sure on the inlet side to the lower pressure at the outlet. This
flash steam builds up above the disc, exerting a pressure tend-
ing to close it. At the same time, flash-steam expansion below
the disc increases the velocity, and decreases the pressure
Thermostatic Trap
Figure 1: Thermostatic trap.
Open
Closed
Thermal Element
Figure 2: Mechanical traps.
Float and Thermostatic Trap
Inverted Bucket Trap
Disc
Seat
Condensate
Locator Pin
Annular Ring
Figure 3: Thermodynamic trap.
38
December 2001|ASHRAE Journal
below the disc. The combination of flash-steam pressure on
top, acting over the large area of the disc, and lower pressure
below snaps the disc closed with an audible popping sound.
Like the inverted bucket trap, disc traps cycle on/off. Disc
traps cannot handle large amounts of air, since high-velocity
air can also lower the pressure enough to snap it shut, and the
small passages in the trap can be clogged by system dirt.
Trap Installation
An analysis of the steam system will determine the most
important trapping requirements. The designer then selects
the trap types that best meet these application requirements.
For a heat exchanger that uses a temperature regulator to modu-
late the steam supply, most designers will choose an F&T trap.
The trap’s modulating action tracks with changes in the con-
densing rate. Because condensate subcooling is not required,
the heat exchanger tube bundle will be protected from damag-
ing water hammer. The trap’s vent element is very important
since heat exchangers of this type often use a vacuum breaker.
As steam condenses, it decreases greatly in volume, which
could induce a vacuum in the heat exchanger shell. This low
pressure at the trap inlet compared to atmospheric pressure at
the trap outlet would reverse the differential pressure required
by the trap, leading to a failure to drain the condensate. The
vacuum breaker opens to introduce air as the vacuum starts to
form ensuring that the pressure in the heat exchanger can never
drop lower than the setting of the vacuum breaker (just slightly
lower than atmospheric pressure in most applications). If the
trap can be installed some distance below the condensate drain
point, there will always be a minimum hydrostatic head to
force condensate out of the heat exchanger.
Ensuring Trap Operation
Industrial applications often can afford to install a visual test
fitting downstream of the trap to verify its operation (Figure 4).
Normal operation of the trap is verified by observing conden-
sate plus flash steam coming from the test valve. If there is no
discharge at all, the trap may be clogged shut, or the float may
Figure 4: Visual test fitting installed downstream of the trap
to verify its operation.
Static
Head
Isolation
Valve
Dirt
Leg
To
Drain
Test
Gravity Return
to Vented Receiver
Isolation
Valve
Trap
be waterlogged and unable to rise. If high-velocity live steam
comes out of the test fitting, the trap has failed open. In heating
systems, it’s often impractical to install a visual test fitting, so
other means for determining the trap status must be used.
A non-contact thermometer is useful for rapid screening of
traps. A large difference between the steam temperature and
the condensate just downstream of the trap could indicate a
failed closed trap. A trap that’s failed open will tend to have
equal temperatures on each side. Since thermostatic traps de-
pend on condensate subcooling, this is a particularly useful
method for testing them.
Simple acoustic tests can be conducted with a stethoscope
from an auto supply store. A failed shut trap will have no flow
noise, while a working trap will make a gurgling sound. A
whistling sound often accompanies loss of steam from the trap.
Some experience is required to tell if the trap is working right.
Conclusion
Steam traps usually are installed well below the equipment
they serve, so it’s easy to neglect and ignore them. If this is
done, they will eventually fail, causing all of the costly conse-
quences outlined here. A frequent, thorough inspection pro-
gram is the best way to ensure that these vital elements are
working properly to yield the highest return from the invest-
ment in the steam system.

December 2001|ASHRAE Journal
An Interview with President Bill Coad
ASHRAE and Building Safety
oncerns about safety in buildings are elevating ASHRAE’s
visibility as the public looks for knowledgeable answers
and help. Examples include recent articles in the New York
Times, Los Angeles Times, Chicago Sun-Times and major news
magazines. ASHRAE President Bill Coad, P.E., Fellow/Life
Member ASHRAE, says this visibility also brings new respon-
sibilities. This point was made during the interview presented
here. Coad also discusses how ASHRAE is responding to con-
cerns about biological and chemical terrorism, and how the role
of the engineer is being affected.
1. What should ASHRAE members tell the general public
about building safety?
It depends on whether the question is general or specific.
Since we are engineers and the experts on the quality of the
air in the built environment, we must realize that what we say
will be taken very seriously by the public. For that
reason we must be very careful not to make state-
ments that mislead and cause people to take ac-
tion that may not apply to their specific case.
In an effort to caution the public and try to pre-
vent them from taking action that could cause more
harm than benefit, I have issued a press release
that basically says that if a person is not acting on
the advice of an engineer skilled in building sys-
tems he/she should:
• Not seal off outdoor air intakes.
• Not turn off supply fans or exhaust fans dur-
ing occupied hours.
• Inspect and service all air filters to assure that they are the
proper type (as specified in the original HVAC system design),
are clean and are properly installed.
• Check belts and drives to assure that supply fans and ex-
haust fans are operating properly.
Regarding specific buildings, many of our ASHRAE mem-
bers are highly skilled in building systems technology and
can advise the public on specific steps to lessen the risk of
chemical or biological contamination. Much of the latest tech-
nology and the knowledge we’ve been developing for improved
indoor air quality are applicable to these phenomena.
2. How will biological/chemical threats change the HVAC
engineer’s role?
It will certainly make the public more aware of the necessity
to listen more seriously to the engineering community. Many
developers and builders have often disregarded sound engi-
neering principles in a misguided effort to reduce costs, and
have accepted the risks associated with substandard perfor-
mance. In a society in which purposeful contamination is a
concern, the risk factor changes from comfort to life safety.
3. What are the issues and challenges?
I believe the paradigm of how we configure our HVAC sys-
tems will shift, and we’ll see a new configuration of ventilat-
ing systems that will allow us to provide a greater level of
performance, and lower energy consumption without signifi-
cant cost burden.
4. How is ASHRAE responding internally (to members) and
externally (to the public at large)?
ASHRAE is approaching this issue very seriously and delib-
erately. On Sept. 12, I shared a message with our membership
on the ASHRAE web site.
I have conferred with the ASHRAE executive committee, and
we are essentially in full agreement that this issue must be ad-
dressed by the Society since our membership is com-
prised of the leading talent in building systems tech-
nology throughout the world. We are also very con-
cerned about the sensitivity of the issue and are do-
ing everything that we can to prevent opportunists
and others who would exacerbate the situation by
taking advantage of the public or inadvertently aid-
ing the perpetrators.
Specifically, we have established the Presiden-
tial Study Group on Health and Safety under Ex-
traordinary Incidents, a small group of leading ex-
perts in all areas of building systems technology
(except structural). This group is studying all is-
sues relating to the health and safety of building occupants
under extraordinary incidents. Their charge is to complete their
work and submit a final report to the executive committee of
the Board of Directors at the January meeting. Assuming all
goes well, the results will be reported to the membership at a
public session in Atlantic City.
5. What do you hope the study group will achieve?
The study group is comprised of experts in virtually all as-
pects of building services, including materials, egress, con-
taminants, fire extinguishing, smoke control, filtration, main-
tenance, envelopes, water supply and energy service systems
(electric and fuel). They will study the experiences of the Sept.
11 attacks and the subsequent biological implantations to
determine the effectiveness of the existing building systems.
Combining this information with their specific expert knowl-
edge and skills, they will make recommendations to the board
of directors concerning the following:
• Steps that managers of existing building facilities can
take to improve the safety of their buildings.
C
Bill Coad
ASHRAE Journal|December 2001
15
• What ASHRAE can do to inform its
members of the steps that can and should
be taken to improve the health and safety
of existing buildings in the case of an
extraordinary event.
• What ASHRAE can encourage its
membership to do in regard to the de-
sign of new facilities to ensure a high
level of safety inside buildings.
• Recommend action regarding the
need for research, standards, codes, for-
mation of technical committees or task
groups and educational programs, or any
other needs to address the subject mat-
ter.
• Identify ongoing work with other or-
ganizations that may be relevant.
ASHRAE Journal|December 2001
27
Robert Kohl, P.E., is senior project manager for
ALC Controls, Atlanta. The project described in
this article was done when the author worked
for Air Analysis, Atlanta.
Commissioning HVAC
Controls Systems
About the Author
N
By Robert Kohl, P.E.
Member ASHRAE
ext in importance to selecting a competent engineer to design
and inspect an HVAC project, every sizeable HVAC project should
have its controls commissioned by a registered Control Systems Engi-
neer (if possible) or at least a professional engineer with demonstrated
HVAC controls experience. The process of controls commissioning is
of primary importance to a properly operating building.
An incomplete commissioning of the
temperature control/building automa-
tion systems compromises the total ben-
efit promised by a thorough and profes-
sional commissioning effort. Overlook-
ing even the smallest of details may re-
sult in sub optimal system operation
plus a large liability risk for the con-
tractor, engineer and owner. Conversely,
a complete and thorough control Func-
tional Performance Testing (FPT) may
uncover many hidden defects of an
HVAC system and controls systems.
Although controls and FPT are only
one of several phases of integrated com-
missioning as defined in ASHRAE Guide-
line 1-1996, HVAC Commissioning Pro-
cess, it is critical to proper control sys-
tems operation and is the focus of this
article. The author asserts that since
proper control systems operation is criti-
cal to proper overall HVAC systems op-
eration, a well designed and thoroughly
administered FPT is a critical component
of integrated commissioning.
This article describes a project where
a thorough FPT of a control installation
in a commercial building uncovered
many deficiencies, some with major li-
ability ramifications for the contractor,
engineer and owner.
Controls Commissioning
The author has found through personal
experience that there are professional,
ethical control contractors and distribu-
tors in our industry that provide excel-
lent customer satisfaction. However, many
building owners routinely accept sub-
stantially substandard controls systems
from their contractors resulting in sub-
stantially suboptimal HVAC systems op-
eration, even in buildings where a formal
commissioning process was performed.
Several reasons exist for this:
1. Plan and specification bid work is
extremely competitive. The low bid
contractor typically has only
enough money to do the job per
plans and specifications even
though the contractor knows this
may not be in the best interest of
the owner. Most control systems are
not designed by the engineer of
record, but by the (low bid) controls
contractor. This leads to a conflict
between cost and quality.
2. The majority of control specifica-
tions do not accurately incorporate
the quality level, coordination with
other equipment, or sequence of
operation that the owner and engi-
neer are trying to accomplish with
the HVAC system. The control
specification and its associated FPT
specification are two places in the
design process that need much im-
provement. Using an unbiased con-
trol systems professional engineer
to design and specify these specifi-
cation sections can help improve
the process.
3. In the author’s experience, most
control FPTs are designed and ad-
ministered by test and balance per-
sonnel, or engineers who lack the
specific and detailed control pro-
gramming, and networking knowl-
edge required to properly commis-
sion today’s modern and complex
control systems. Most of the FPT
documents reviewed by the author
require the testing of only a frac-
tion of what is necessary to ensure
a properly functioning system.
4. Owners do not have the on staff ex-
28
December 2001|ASHRAE Journal
pertise to thoroughly test modern building automation
systems (BAS). An experienced professional is required
for this function.
Building owners are incurring great expense as a result of
incomplete testing in the following areas:
1. Inefficient equipment operation leading to high en-
ergy costs.
2. Inefficient personnel due to incomplete training.
3. Labor and material costs incurred by the owner’s person-
nel in correcting the control systems deficiencies they
find due to occupants complaints or “fault occurrences”
during the first few years of operation.
4. The owner’s personnel not having good as-builts of the
controls and the control sequences so that they can prop-
erly manage their plant.
5. Equipment and control system breakdown and costly
repair due to improper operation.
6. Hidden personnel costs (productivity, absenteeism, and
turnover) due to improper ventilation and/or tempera-
ture control.
7. Building occupant complaints.
Although the commissioning specifications may call for
the control systems to be tested in every phase and step of
every mode, they are not being tested in that way. The FPT is
incomplete if it does not test:
1. Safeties in every mode, i.e., in manual run mode as well
as auto mode.
2. Signals to and from the fire alarm, security and entry
systems.
3. Sequences of operation step by step in every mode and
possible situation.
4. The operation of all control loops under actual operat-
ing conditions.
5. The interlocked operation of all equipment (i.e., the op-
eration of starters in manual and off modes as well as
auto mode, damper end switch interlock, etc.).
6. Where the BAS performs computations, the actual com-
putation of any formulas and simulation of actual con-
ditions to check the BAS computations.
7. Review of BAS programs for errors and omissions.
8. Hard copy graphs of trend logs of most, if not all, opera-
tional parameters.
In other words, commissioning should test every conceiv-
able life-safety scenario and every conceivable operational
scenario that the system would encounter and document this
testing with printed graphs of trend logs.
The following project is representative of the level of defi-
ciencies that may be bought by an owner without performing
adequate FPT.
Actual Case History
A large 1.5 million ft2 (139 350 m2) commercial building was
renovated to enlarge personnel areas. The mechanical contractor
involved was one of the better contractors in the area. The BAS
vendor, a major control manufacturer, was the sole source for the
controls because of its extensive existing system in the build-
ing. The BAS specification, was prepared by an experienced and
highly regarded engineering firm, was more explicit than most
BAS specifications and it included requirements for FPT.
The HVAC system consisted of a variable air volume (VAV)
air-handling unit (AHU A) with speed control of the supply air
fan from supply air static pressure, and 10 VAV terminal units.
An airside economizer was provided with an airflow monitor-
ing station in the outside air duct, including an array of veloc-
ity pressure pick up tubes that could provide fairly accurate
input for air volume calculation by the BAS.
The sequence of operation required maintenance of mini-
mum outside air requirements for each zone by calculation of
the required minimum outdoor air cfm per ANSI/ASHRAE Stan-
dard 62-1989, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality,
and control of it with a PID loop that modulated outdoor air
and return air dampers. For further information on calculating
the required fraction of outdoor air, see ASHRAE Journal, Sept.
2001, “Ventilation Control Strategies.”
The balance of the HVAC system consisted of two single
zone AHUs (Units B and C) with variable supply air volume
and two zones of straight reheat each.
After the system was installed, completely checked, tested and
balanced, and turned over to the owner as complete and in con-
formance with specifications, the author performed the FPT. Prior
to this testing, the HVAC and controls systems were functioning
and maintaining marginally acceptable conditions in the spaces.
Because the author was employed directly by the owner and
did not have to competitively bid on the commissioning speci-
fication, an FPT was designed that exceeded the specified
requirements. Actual control loop parameters were evaluated
with trend logs (TL) to check for hunting or excessive droop.
The control vendor’s equipment data sheets, programs, and
wiring diagram submittals were reviewed to verify that control
equipment and design met the specifications.
Deficiencies
The FPT identified the folling deficiencies. (Note that the
following comments are taken from the actual FPT report.)
AHU A
1. Minimum ventilation calculations are not working and
vary widely from the specified calculations.
2. Minimum outdoor air (OA) control loop is hunting and
unstable.
3. Outside air airflow monitoring station is out of calibra-
tion. (A traverse was run to discover this.)
4. Economizer mixed air temperature control loop is un-
stable. (See comments below.)
5. Minimum and maximum outside air dampers are not op-
erating in unison when system is in economizer mode.
Commissioning
ASHRAE Journal|December 2001
29
6. Chilled water valve is not spring return as specified.
7. Supply air smoke damper stays open on unit shutdown.
NFPA 90A-1996, 4.3.2, requires that smoke dampers close
whenever the unit is off.
8. Return air duct detector is not functional.
9. Fire alarm programming between duct detectors and shut-
down relay has not been done.
10. There is no hardwired shutdown interlock between duct
detectors and variable frequency drives as specified.
11. There is no hardwired interlock as specified between smoke
dampers end switches and variable frequency drives.
12. There is no level two-freeze alarm sequence. (Note that
the owner required a three-tier (three-level) freeze pro-
tection sequence for this project.)
13. On level three-freeze alarm, there is no interlock to
open chilled water valve to coil flow or start the chilled
water pump.
14. There is only 20 ft (6 m) of freezestat element to protect
48 ft2 (4 m2) of coil face. Good practice recommends 1 ft
(0.3 m) of element per 1 ft2 (0.09 m2) of coil, and espe-
cially where there is electric preheat that is probably
face split for staging. Consider adding a second freezestat
to help protect the cooling coil from freezing. (Cold,
stratified outside air can freeze a coil section even smaller
than 1ft2 [0.09 m2].)
15. Preheat is controlled from a separate setpoint set colder
than supply air temperature setpoint. Specification sec-
tion, P. 22, requires that preheat be controlled from the
supply air temperature setpoint. If these are to be con-
trolled from separate setpoints, then there should be soft-
ware interlock to keep the pre-heat and cooling from
fighting. We suggest that if pre-heat is on, then cooling
be locked out.
16. Optimum start feature does not work.
AHUs B and C
The following deficiencies from AHU A above also apply to
AHUs B and C: 4, 5, 10, 11, 12, 14 and 15 plus the follow-
ing additional deficiencies.
1. Owner can select to control space from either sensor or
average of both. This is not owner adjustable weighted
control as specified.
2. BAS controls variable frequency drive down to 50%
speed. However 60% is the minimum specified.
3. Motion detector inputs causing excessive fluctuations
of minimum outside air setpoint. During unoccupied
operation, the outside air damper opens and closes ev-
ery time someone walks through the room
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
30
December 2001|ASHRAE Journal
4. Chilled water valve is cycling excessively. See trend log
print of 8-27-98. This condition will result in premature
actuator failure and excessive energy usage. Control loop
should be tuned.
5. Trend log printouts show an average space temperature
variation of from about 76°F (24°C) at 6:30 a.m. to about
73°F (23°C) at noon. This +3°F (1.5°C) variation is too
much for an AHU being controlled by a BAS system
with supply air temperature reset from space tempera-
ture. The space temperature should not vary more than
±0.5°F (0.25°C) from setpoint.
6. Return air duct detector did not actuate fire alarm relay
at local control panel.
AHU C
The following deficiencies also were noted.
1. Chilled water valve does not shut to coil as specified
when return air temperature is below lockout setpoint
(69°F [20°C]).
Graphics
1. Terminal unit ID numbers not included.
2. AHU B. There is no duct smoke detector alarm as speci-
fied at console.
3. AHU B. There is no Level 1 freeze alarm at console. Lev-
els 2 and 3 freeze sequences do not exist.
4. AHU C. There is no duct smoke detector alarm as speci-
fied at console.
5. AHU C. There is no Level 1 freeze alarm at console. Lev-
els 2 and 3 freeze sequences do not exist.
Some of these deficiencies (i.e., the life-safety and IAQ is-
sues) could cause a major liability risk for the owner. The lack
of good control of outside air volume could result in excess
energy consumption or poor indoor air quality. The outdoor
air calculation and control problems were not correctable given
the level of BAS equipment the vendor had supplied.
The vendor supplied an inexpensive VAV box volume control-
ler to sense the outdoor air (OA) cfm. The lack of processing
capacity of the local controllers meant that the cfm calculations
had to be done at the central processor (CP). Due to the slow local
area network (9600 baud), the delays in collecting the zones’
ventilation requirements made the data useless as real-time infor-
mation. Ventilation requirements in each zone were observed to
change considerably before the information was collected for all
zones, the calculations performed, and the revised outside air
volume setpoint was sent to the outside air PID controller.
The PID loop controlling the OA damper was in the central
processor. This control loop was not operating correctly due to
excessive dead time (dead time is the time interval between
the point in time a corrective action is taken by the control
system and when the result of the action is sensed by the con-
trol system). By the time the velocity transmitter relayed the
cfm to the CP, the calculation was done and the correction
relayed back to the AHU controller, the OA cfm had changed
considerably. A hardware upgrade to provide local processing
power would have been necessary to correct these problems.
Although the specification did not call for specific levels of
control hardware, it seemed obvious that whatever level of
hardware was required to perform the specified calculations
and control should have been estimated and supplied by the
control contractor. Had a knowledgeable control systems en-
gineer been hired to review the original submittals of the con-
trol contractor, this scenario might have been avoided.
Corrections
The lack of life-safety shutdown and many of the control
deficiencies were corrected. As for the outside air control and
freeze protection deficiencies, none of those were corrected.
The controls vendor insisted they had installed those properly.
The owner was not willing to risk alienating his BAS sup-
plier by demanding the correction of those deficiencies. It was
pointed out to the owner that the system was several years old
and in imminent danger of being orphaned by the supplier
regardless of his relationship with them. It was also pointed
out that in case of IAQ complaints and possible workmans
compensation claims and litigation, the owner could not prove
adequate OA cfm as required and specified.
In spite of this feedback, the owner decided to accept those
controls “as is” and not demand correction by the contractor.
They at least know where to start if complaints occur in the
future. For control commissioning to be of full benefit, owners
must insist that all deficiencies be corrected.
Conclusions
In the author’s opinion, most if not all control problems will
be avoided by owners taking the following precautions:
1. Incorporate integrated commissioning as described in
ASHRAE Guideline 1-1996 into design specifications,
including thorough and detailed functional performance
testing.
2. Use a commissioning authority beginning with the con-
ceptual phase of the project.
3. Involve a controls professional in the project beginning
with the conceptual phase and extending throughout the
project including the functional performance testing.
4. Prequalifying local BAS contractors/distributors with a
proven track record of professionalism and customer sat-
isfaction. In this respect, the local branch offices of the
large control companies must be judged on their own
merit as if they were independent contractors.
Although controls and FPT are only a part of integrated
commissioning, in the author’s opinion they are of primary
importance. Since controls are the “nerves and brains” of the
HVAC system, owners disregard controls commisioning at
their own peril.
44
December 2001|ASHRAE Journal
By George C. Briley, P.E.,
Fellow/Life Member ASHRAE
H
A Primer on Plate Freezers
orizontal plate freezers (HPFs) have been in use for about
70 years (with the first ones labeled as “Birdseye Frosters”)
and some of them are still in operation. These small freezers
were loaded by placing the boxes of vegetables on trays and
manually inserting between plates. The frosters have been used
to freeze products from peas, corn and lima beans to shrimp.
The modern HPFs are fully automatic and can freeze up to
8,000 lb/h (1000 g/s), depending on package thickness. Fro-
zen dinners are the predominant type of product frozen in
HPFs. Also, ice cream in square packages is frozen in this type
of freezer to keep the packages square.
HPFs consist of a series of refrigerated plates that usually
are enclosed in an insulated cabinet. As the plates are raised
and lowered hydraulically, pressure is maintained on the pack-
age to ensure complete surface contact and to keep the pack-
ages flat and square. The product is placed on each of the
plates or stations when in an open position. When all of the
packages are loaded, the plates are lowered hydraulically with
continuous pressure and then the freezing process begins.
As the refrigerant circulates through the plates, heat is ab-
sorbed from the bottom of the package above it and from the
top of the package immediately under it. This is “double con-
tact freezing.” The extruded plate is circuited to give optimum
heat transfer over the entire freezing surface. The plate’s flat
surfaces ensure uniform flat packages after freezing.
In the past, freezer plates were made of steel. Today, ex-
truded aluminum is the metal of choice because it has better
heat transfer properties.
Horizontal Plate Freezer Operation
Operating on a “first in, first out” basis, packages are routed
by conveyor from the filler/packaging equipment to the infeed
conveyor. A sensing wheel signals when a complete row of
packaged product is in position and activates the automatic
loader/unloader device.
A full-length pusher blade sweeps the row of packaged prod-
uct into the front of the freezer to a predetermined depth. Suc-
ceeding rows of unfrozen packages are loaded into the first
station until it is completely filled. The next freezing station
is automatically moved up to position for product loading.
When the last station is filled, the freezer automatically re-
cycles and all stations, as a single unit, are lowered to the
bottom of the freezer.
When the product in the first station has had sufficient resi-
dence time to be completely frozen, unfrozen product enter-
ing the first station discharges the frozen product through
simple displacement.
The unit can accept boxes up to 5 in. (125 mm) thick (ice
cream-square half gallons [2 L]), down to 0.75 and 1 in. (19
and 25 mm) thick packages. Most wet-pack vegetables, i.e.,
broccoli, cauliflower, etc., are frozen in automatic HPFs.
The horizontal plate freezer is the best choice when com-
pared to blast freezing because the packages are always square.
There is no swelling or disfiguration of the package. Thus, the
packages fit perfectly into a master carton.
It also is the least expensive way to freeze packaged prod-
ucts as there are no fans involved with their attendant extra
refrigeration requirements, i.e., every 4 hp (3 kW) adds 1 ton
(3.5 kW) to the refrigeration load.
An automatic horizontal plate freezer.
This article is the first in a series discussing mechanical food
chilling and freezing systems.
ASHRAE Journal|December 2001
45
To obtain proper performance, the HPF must be piped cor-
rectly. The ammonia overfeed rate of 16 to 20 to 1 requires engi-
neered piping or there will be a major temperature difference
between the top and bottom plate. It can vary as much as 17°F
(8.5°C) at –40°F (–40°C) suction, if the refrigerant piping is
overhead, i.e., the bottom plate would be at –23°F (–31°C).
Vertical Plate Freezer Operation
The manual vertical plate freezer (VPF) produces slabs of fro-
zen product in varying dimensions. These VPF units freeze prod-
ucts in bulk such as offal, cream, juices, fruit pulp, blood, etc.
Plates are arranged vertically and allowed to move in a
lateral plane.
The openings between the freezing stations are sealed by
the carrier, which holds the product. The carrier is placed in the
load (down) position.
The plates are then mechanically (hydraulically) closed to a
fixed dimension, often three, and the product is loaded be-
tween the plates.
Recirculated ammonia refrigerant is then applied through
the freezing plates until the product is frozen to the required
temperature (usually 10°F to 0°F [–12°C to –17°C]).
When the product has been frozen, hot gas is introduced
into the plates to facilitate rapid defrosting. The plates then are
mechanically moved laterally to their fully open position, thus
releasing the blocks.
The carriers are then moved upward by the hydraulic sys-
tem, which moves the frozen blocks out from between the plates.
The method of removing the frozen blocks from the carrier can
be varied to suit the particular requirements of the plant.
Refrigerant piping for VPFs is even more critical as the freezer
is harvested every 90 to 120 minutes and hot gas must be
introduced into the plates gradually.
Little or no weight loss occurs during the freezing cycle.
Products, frozen in vertical plate freezers are of superior qual-
ity as the ice crystals that are formed are small, thus little cell
damage occurs. The cost to freeze is the lowest possible.
Elimination of expensive cartons normally used for blast
freezing saves money in initial cost, eliminates the need for
storage space for the boxes, reduces shipping costs, eliminates
box makeup and handling costs and cold storage space re-
quirements are reduced. The end user saves money by elimi-
nating the carton handling and disposal problem.
George C. Briley, P.E., is the president of Technicold Ser-
vices, San Antonio.
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
ASHRAE Journal|December 2001
31
The Impact of Airtightness
On System Design
About the Author
I
Wagdy Anis, AIA, is a principal and director of
Technical Resources at Shepley Bulfinch Richardson
and Abbott, Boston.
By Wagdy A.Y. Anis, AIA
t is commonly believed that infiltration is due to poorly weather-
stripped doors and windows. Although this is partly true, the fact is
80% or more of infiltration is due to the many imperfections that are
designed or built into exterior envelopes. “Will we have enough fresh
air to breathe?” is a concern about air-tightening the envelope.
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-1999, Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air
Quality has taken care of that problem. Doesn’t air tightening cause
sick buildings? To quote, Joseph Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Member
ASHRAE,1 “in order to control air, you must first contain it.”
Air pressure acting on building enve-
lopes can wreak havoc with building per-
formance if not properly understood and
adequately designed for. Uncontrolled air
pressure across the building envelope and
within the building itself can cause in-
filtration and exfiltration that overpower
HVAC systems. By disrupting the HVAC
design, pressures can cause discomfort
and create infection control and indoor
air quality problems.
In heating climates, exfiltrating air car-
ries with it water vapor and lost energy.
The water vapor condenses and causes
many problems, from wetting to bacte-
rial growth and deterioration of the build-
ing envelope.
In cooling climates, water vapor is
carried in with the infiltrating air, caus-
ing condensation, mold, and bacterial
growth. Moisture-laden air can travel
within interstitial spaces due to nega-
tive pressures inside those spaces. The
moisture can condense in strange
places like interior walls and ceilings
that are connected to the building en-
velope.
Leakage Characteristics2
Air leakage can occur through pores
in materials, cracks, holes, or other open-
ings. Flow is produced by a pressure dif-
ference that provides the energy to over-
come friction and other losses. Air leak-
age transfers heat, water vapor, smoke,
odors, dust, and other pollutants, either
from outdoors into the building or from
sources within the building.
Airflow characteristics vary according
to the size and shape of the opening. Long
paths with small cross-sections may ex-
hibit laminar flow and have resistance
proportional to velocity. Larger holes
may act like orifices with resistance to
flow varying with the square of velocity.
Usually, different kinds of openings con-
tribute to the total leakage. It is not prac-
tical to identify, measure, or calculate
each individually. Overall flow rates for
the aggregate of openings take the form:
(

tween 0.5 and 1.0. An exponent of 0.65
represents many cases of wall and win-
dow leakage.
Figure 1 illustrates the relationship
between pressure difference and flow
through building openings.
Three major sources of air pressure on
buildings are wind pressure, stack pres-
sure, and HVAC fan pressure. Two minor
pressures are changing barometric air
pressure and temperature differentials
across the building envelope. They are
inconsequential relative to the three
major pressures.
Air Leakage From Wind Pressure
Although peak pressures are important
for structural calculations, mean values are
more appropriate for computing infiltra-
tion rates. Time-averaged surface pressures
are proportional to the wind velocity given
by Bernoulli’s equation, as follows:
g

The difference between pressure on the envelope surface
and local outdoor atmospheric pressure at the same level in an
undisturbed wind approaching the building is given by
v

C
p
⋅

(3)
where
ps
=
difference in pressure, lb/ft2
Cp
=
local wind pressure coefficient (depends on ter-
rain).
Of the three major air pressures, wind is usually the greatest.
If the wind hits the building broadside, air infiltrates on the
windward side and exfiltrates on the other three sides and
through the roof. If the wind hits at an angle, it positively
pressurizes two sides, and air exfiltrates on the two leeward
sides and through the roof.
Infiltrating air is unconditioned for temperature and mois-
ture content and can contain pollutants. It causes discomfort
and can cause imbalances in spaces such as patient isolation
rooms, protected environment rooms, or chemical storage ar-
eas that are designed for controlled pressure, thus compromis-
ing pollutant control.
Chapters 16 and 26 of the 2001 ASHRAE Handbook—Fun-
damentals quantify wind pressures on buildings and their ef-
fects on mechanical systems.
Stack Effect
Have you ever approached the front door of a building in
winter and found the door really difficult to pull open, and
then it opened with a “whoosh?” This condition is due to stack
effect. Stack effect or “chimney effect” in buildings is caused
by the difference in weight of the column of conditioned air
inside the building versus the air outside the building. This
difference in weight creates a pressure difference across the
building envelope.
Absolute air pressure changes more rapidly with height out-
doors than indoors, causing the pressure differential. Mechani-
cal and elevator shafts, stairs, and atria connect the bottom of
the building to the top and transfer the stack pressure at each
floor to the envelope. Figure 2 illustrates stack effect.
The taller the building, the higher the stack pressure. In
heating climates, air exfiltrates at the top and is replaced by air
infiltrating at the bottom of the building. In cooling climates,
the opposite occurs. Cold air coming in can cause discomfort
at the lower floors and overpower the heating system. In a 40-
story building, with a 13 ft (4 m) story height, temperatures of
20°F (–7°C) outdoors and 70°F (21°C) indoors, and assuming
openings of equal area at the top and bottom of the building,
the infiltration stack pressure at street level (and exfiltration
at the top of the building) is 0.4 in. w.g. (100 Pa).
The design of garage exhaust systems needs to account for
stack pressure in the building above. Stack pressure can ex-
ceed exhaust fan static pressure. If it does, a garage under a
Figure 1: Airflow
rate is determined
by the size of the
hole and the pres-
sure difference.
Figure 2: Stack effect.
Figure 3: Combined wind, stack and fan pressure.
∆ Pressure
Difference
Flow Rate (cfm)
Area of Opening
Wind Pressure
Heating Climate Stack Pressure
HVAC Fan Pressure
Combined Effect
ASHRAE Journal|December 2001
33
building can be a source of pollutants that are sucked into the
building. A good passive solution to the problem would be to
use vestibules and door weather stripping to isolate and com-
partmentalize the garage from the building above at stairs,
elevator shafts, and other physical connections that allow this
stack pressure to connect the building to the garage. Compart-
mentalizing the building horizontally into individual stories
or small groups of a few stories and disconnecting them from
the shafts fools the building into acting like a stack of single-
story or low-rise buildings, thereby reducing stack effect.
Outside air and exhaust fans in multistory apartment build-
ings often are not selected to account for stack pressure. Multi-
story systems with vertical ducts or shafts, such as bathroom
exhaust systems in hotels, have been known to fail miserably,
with the bathroom exhaust from lower floors being sucked
into the bathrooms at the upper stories of the building. If pas-
sive compartmentalization is not part of the design, individual
systems that serve each floor might be necessary to overcome
stack effect. Controls that include pressure sensors and vari-
able speed drives then can adjust fan speed to account for
changing pressures on the envelope. Supply systems with ver-
tical duct distribution shafts have to work against varying pres-
sures during the year. There seems to be no easy solution that
allows the original balancing to work reliably at all times.
Have you ever noticed how some brick buildings will turn
whitish with efflorescence at the top parapet? That is due to air
escaping at the juncture of the top floor’s walls and roof. Mois-
ture in the exfiltrating air condenses on the back or within the
brick, dissolving salts from the masonry. The moisture dries at
the surface of the brick, leaving salts on the surface. In the spring,
rain dissolves the salts again and the whitish stain disappears.
Besides efflorescence, moist air exfiltrating at the top of the
building can cause damage by decay and corrosion, spalling
of masonry and stone, and bacterial growth. Inter-floor pres-
sure differentials can also disrupt designs sensitive to accurate
pressure relationships in non-compartmentalized buildings.

The neutral pressure level is the elevation where airflow
changes from exfiltration to infiltration. In buildings with equal
leakage area at the top and the bottom of the building, the
neutral pressure level occurs at mid-height of the building.
The location of the neutral plane is affected by the relative
area of leakage at the top versus the bottom of the building. In
most buildings, more leakage happens at the top, due to lou-
vers in mechanical rooms, elevator shaft open smoke vent lou-
vers, even weep holes in skylight curbs, and especially the
wall-to roof connection, which most architects do not attempt
to design to be airtight.
The following equation can be used to combine wind and
stack infiltration:

Fan Pressure
HVAC engineers often design buildings to be under a net
positive pressure. This reportedly helps keep pollutants and
untreated air out of the building. The uncertainties of infiltra-

Type 1 Data: Test performed on whole building; total envelope area (includ-
ing below grade) used to calculate NLR at 0.3 in. w.g. (75 Pa)
Type 2 Data: Test performed on whole building using different test pres-

Table 1: Mean Normalized Leakage Rate (NLR) at 0.3 in. w.g.

34
December 2001|ASHRAE Journal
tion through the envelope may drive this common practice.
The results are hit or miss. Recent studies and testing on whole
building air leakage are summarized in Table 1. Air leakage
rates due to infiltration are all over the place.3
In heating-dominated climates, net positive fan pressure has
the effect of driving moisture-laden air through the many un-
avoidable imperfections that are built into walls, floors and
roofs. Water condenses in the winter, damaging the envelope.
From an envelope durability standpoint, buildings should be
pressurized slightly negative in heating climates and positive
in cooling climates. What is practical from an HVAC perfor-
mance standpoint is sometimes inconsistent with the durabil-
ity needs of the envelope.
Fan pressure is caused by design or by accident. One four-
story academic laboratory building the author recently re-
viewed in the winter was running under a negative pressure of
0.3 in. w.g. (75 Pa) on the second floor. When it rained, water
was sucked into every crevice in the envelope. The building
leaked like a sieve, aided by less than adequate flashings. The
HVAC system could not deal with all the infiltration, and people
were cold. The negative pressure was caused primarily by an
inadequate balance between the supply and exhaust.
Figure 3 illustrates the combined influence of stack effect,
wind pressure, and fan pressure.
Recommendations
So what is the answer? Prudent building envelope design
will take air pressures into consideration. An airtight building
envelope is crucial to proper HVAC system function. Horizon-
tal compartmentalization of the floors and isolation from shafts
is essential to control stack effect and the spread of smoke and
fire. Air barrier design technology is essential to success. De-
signers can take the following actions:
1. Design the exterior envelope and all its components to
withstand the combined design wind, stack, and fan pressures
in an airtight manner. Massachusetts, funded by U.S. Depart-
ment of Energy (DOE) and the utilities, is providing free enve-
lope energy code training including design guidance on air
barrier technology and educational details.4 Specifications
for air barriers systems are available from the Air Barrier Asso-
ciation of America (ABAA)5 as well as details of its Quality
Assurance Program, qualified contractors, etc.
2. Design an air barrier system into the building envelope
that can take this pressure, both positive and negative, without
displacement or failure. This will help control air infiltration
and exfiltration. For areas within a building with significantly
different climates, include an air barrier system into the separa-
tion between the two areas, such as between pools and offices,
or humidity-controlled areas and adjacent uncontrolled areas.
3. Separate shafts (elevators, stairs, ducts and atria) from
the floors they serve by airtight assemblies. Provide vesti-
bules and gasket doors and access panels to control transfer
of stack pressure.
4. Separate pollutant areas such as photocopy rooms,
chemical or cleaning storage areas, toilets, and garages with
gasketed doors, and make the surrounding partitions airtight
at the deck and floor.
Code Requirements
When Standard 90.1 was updated for release in 1999, the
drafters recognized that an opportunity was being missed. Sec-
tion 5.2.3, Air Leakage, notes: “It is difficult to specify in great
detail the ways to limit air leakage, which is unfortunate given
its potential significance. Minimizing air leakage is important
to maintaining comfort, enabling the mechanical system to meet
loads, and maintaining building pressurization in case of a fire,
in addition to the benefits of reduced energy bills.”
So we continue with energy code requirements, including
ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1-1999, Energy Standard
for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings and The
International Energy Conservation Code (IECC 2000), which
are extremely vague when it comes to air tightening the build-
ing envelope. Air tightening is typically called for by requir-
ing caulking, gasketing, weather stripping and stuffing crev-
ices and cracks. That is an outdated and naïve approach to a
major problem in buildings today.
With energy sources becoming problematic worldwide, it is
an unsustainable position. The 2001 ASHRAE Handbook—Fun-
damentals, Chapters 23 and 24, clearly calls for the solution,
requiring building envelopes to be designed with an air retarder
or barrier. The Envelope Design Guidelines for Federal Office
Buildings: Thermal Integrity and Airtightness6 also contains
clear details and requirements for air barriers. The Building En-
vironment and Thermal Envelope Council (BETEC), division
of the National Institute for Building Sciences has held three
symposia, Air Barriers I, II and III, from 1999-2001, sponsored
by DOE and Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL), to draw
national attention to the problem of air leakage in buildings
and how to take care of it using air barrier technology. The pro-
moted technology continues to be ignored in the codes and
therefore, in actual practice. It is the author’s belief that until air
barriers are clearly required by code, it will be business as usual
in designing and building buildings that leak air.
An “air retarder” or “air barrier” is a component of the build-
ing envelope that the designer selects in each envelope system
to be airtight. It is taped or otherwise made airtight. It has to be
structurally supported to withstand the positive and negative
pressures on the envelope. It is joined with the air barrier compo-

ASHRAE Journal|December 2001
35
nent of the adjacent system in a flexible and airtight manner,
using membranes, sealants, etc. The National Building Code of
Canada (NBC) has had these requirements for 16 years. Section
5.4.1, Air Barrier Systems, of NBC 1995 requires a minimum air
barrier material airtightness of 0.02 L/s·m2 at 75 Pa (the airtight-
ness of a sheet of 0.5 in. (12.5 mm) unpainted gypsum board).
Earlier this year, Massachusetts adopted a new energy code.
Section 1304.3.1, Air Barriers, imposes a clear requirement for
air barriers in the exterior envelope. The requirement is similar
to the Canadian Code and has the same value of airtightness
for materials that qualify as an air barrier (0.004 cfm at 1.57/ft2
or 0.3 in. w.g. [75 Pa]). Massachusetts opted not to define
“airtight” since airtightness is relative, and the building and
design industries in Massachusetts are new to the concepts.
Air barrier materials of all envelope systems must be con-
nected in an airtight and flexible manner, windows and doors
to walls, walls to the roof and foundations, and all penetra-
tions of the air barrier made airtight. The end result is a build-
ing with far reduced air leakage and more durable, energy
efficient and healthier environment.
The Canadian Building Materials Centre has developed a
testing protocol for air barrier “systems.” A “system” is a wall
connected to a foundation with penetrations such as conduits,
a window, etc. The system is then tested under certain pressures
and for varying lengths of time. The appendix to the Canadian
Building Code suggests that the airtightness of the building
envelope can be more or less relaxed depending on the interior
humidity levels. The numbers relate to the air leakage of the
air barrier “system.” This does not relate to the leakage that can
happen at significant flaws built into the building envelope. It
envisions diffuse air leakage. From the point of view of the
integrity of the envelope, it does not make sense to average
out significant flaws over a large area or over the whole build-
ing. From the point of view of the HVAC design engineer,
perhaps, but not necessarily.
There is an ongoing international debate as to what may, or
may not be achievable in whole building airtightness. As seen
in Table 1, which includes some Canadian buildings that were
intentionally designed with air barriers under the C2000 pro-
gram, the numbers in the Canadian code Appendix (0.02 cfm/ft2
at 0.3 in. w.g. [0.10 L/s·m2 at 75 Pa] for interior RH between
27% – 55%) were not achieved. Recent modeling7 done in
collaboration between NRC and VTT Finland refutes the maxi-
mum air leakage rates listed in the appendix of the NBC 1995
that are linked to the interior relative humidity. The newer
recommendations8 are to link the allowable air leakage rates
of an air barrier “system” to the permeability of the exterior
cladding. Table 2 presents these recommendations. The mod-
eling was done with an interior winter RH of 35%.
Until the dust settles and whole building testing becomes
more readily available and meaningful (concentrated flaws cre-
ate concentrated problems) the question will remain whether
quantifying whole building airtightness is meaningful from an
envelope design perspective. Certainly for the HVAC engineer,
the ability to predict the building’s airtightness is important from
a design standpoint. Deliberately designing and building enve-
lopes with care and with a target of airtightness is the best goal.
Where does the architect start and the engineer stop in build-
ing envelope design? A colleague, Fred Wacjs, Member
ASHRAE,9 tells me (partly because it’s true and partly because I
am an architect) that the building envelope is part of the me-
chanical system. The building envelope is designed by architects
who, most of the time, do not realize the interaction between the
envelope and the mechanical system. It is then built by many
contracting trades to what they think is required by the design,
without particular concern for airtightness. For this reason, more
education and knowledge in this area is needed, and an informed
collaboration is essential between all, particularly when the de-
sired result is the air tightening of the building envelope.
References
1. Lstiburek, J.L. Building Science Corporation, Westford, Mass.
2. Hutcheon, N.B. and G.O.P. Handegord. 1983. Building Science
for a Cold Climate.
3. Air Leakage Characteristics, Test Methods and Specifications For

4. To view resources, air barrier details, self-help tools and training
information, www.state.ma.us/bbrs/energy.htm.
5. Air Barrier Association of America, www.airbarrier.org.
6. Persily, A.K. Envelope Design Guidelines for Federal Office Build-

ment of Commerce, National Institute of Standards and Technology,

7. Ojanen, T. and Kumaran, K. Effect of Exfiltration on the
Hygrothermal Behaviour of a Residential Wall Assembly: Results From

8. Canadian Construction Materials Centre (CCMC) Technical Guide.
9. Manager, Field Support, NE Utilities Service Co., Hartford, Conn.

ASHRAE Journal|January 2002
15
Moisture in Buildings
About the Author
H
By John F. Straube, Ph.D.
Associate Member ASHRAE
VAC designers must consider and deal with moisture in almost all
of their work. The moisture most building designers consider is in
vapor form inside ducts, conditioned spaces, or outdoors. Although
this may be the form of moisture that most interests a design engineer,
understanding the source of this vapor is important, as this understand-
ing allows for more robust and economical designs, better risk man-
agement, more accurate diagnostics and improved forensics.
John F. Straube, Ph.D., is a professor at the
Department of Civil Engineering and School of
Architecture, University of Waterloo, Waterloo,
ON, Canada.
This article briefly describes the most
common sources of moisture in build-
ings and introduces the processes by
which this moisture moves in and through
buildings.
Moisture Problems
Moisture has long been important to
building designers since it is the single
most important agent of building dete-
rioration. For example, moisture, from
whatever source, is involved in
•
Electrochemical corrosion of metal
components such as HVAC equipment,
ducts, structural framing, reinforcing bars,
masonry anchors, etc.;
•
The chemical deterioration and dis-
solution of materials such as gypsum
sheathing, ceiling tiles, wood products,
and damaging chemical processes such
as carbonation and alkali-aggregate re-
action;
•
Freeze-thaw deterioration of con-
crete, stone, and masonry;
•
Discoloration (staining, “dusting,”
irregular wetting, etc.) of building fin-
ishes;
•
Volume changes (swelling, warp-
ing, shrinkage) that can cause degrada-
tion of appearance, structural failure,
cracking, etc.; and
•
The growth of biological forms, in-
cluding mold, plants, dust mites, etc.
Moisture-related biological growth
(mostly fungal) has taken on new sig-
nificance recently because such growth
can have a major effect on occupant
health (IAQ), structural capacity, and ap-
pearance.
For a moisture-related problem to oc-
cur, it is necessary for at least four condi-
tions to be satisfied:
1. A moisture source must be avail-
able,
2. There must be a route or means for
this moisture to travel,
3. There must be some driving force
to cause moisture movement, and
4. The material(s) involved must be
susceptible to moisture damage.
To avoid a moisture problem, one
could in theory choose to eliminate any
one of the four conditions. In reality, it is
practically impossible to remove all
moisture sources, to create buildings with
no imperfections, or to remove all forces
driving moisture movement. It is also
uneconomical to use only materials that
are not susceptible to moisture damage.
Hence, in practice it is often advanta-
geous to address two or more of these
prerequisites so as to reduce the prob-
ability of having a problem.
Therefore, controlling or managing
moisture and reducing the risk of mois-
ture-related problems by judicious de-
sign, assembly and material choices is
the most practical approach for design-
ing buildings and conditioning systems.
The Moisture Balance
If a balance between wetting and dry-
ing is maintained, moisture will not ac-
cumulate over time, and moisture-related
problems are unlikely (Figure 1). There-
fore, the extent and duration of wetting,
storage and drying must always be con-
sidered when assessing the risk of mois-
ture damage.
Most moisture control strategies tend
to reduce the amount of wetting by, for
example, increasing the airtightness and
vapor resistance of the enclosure, reduc-
ing the volume of rain water penetration
and absorption, etc. However, it has be-
come generally accepted that most build-
ing construction will not be perfect, thus
wetting will occur. Chilled water piping
may not be perfectly insulated at all
joints; windows have a propensity to
leak; etc. Therefore, the provision of
greater drying potential and storage ca-
pacity have begun to receive more at-
Moisture Control Series
16
January 2002|ASHRAE Journal
tention. These are powerful, and often overlooked moisture
control design strategies that are often present in buildings
that have survived for centuries. Finally, increasing moisture
tolerance through intelligent design of building location, ori-
entation, geometry, HVAC design, etc. is often the least expen-
sive and best approach (although it must be considered very
early in the concept stage).
The major wetting and drying processes and the moisture
transport mechanisms involved in the movement of moisture
into and out of the enclosure are summarized in Figure 2.
Moisture Sources
There are four primary sources of moisture in buildings. It is
useful to categorize them as:
1. Liquid water, from precipitation (rain and melting snow)
or plumbing leaks;
2. Water vapor, from the exterior and from activities and
processes within the building;
3. Liquid and vapor from the soil adjoining the building;
and
4. Moisture built-in with the materials of construction or
brought in with goods and people.
Piping leaks and rain penetration are obvious sources of
water that must be avoided. Rain deposition on roofs is usu-
ally of the order of several hundred to one thousand kg/m2
(200 lb/ft2) in most climates. Walls typically receive about
25% to 50% of this load.1 If only a very small fraction of this
precipitation leaks into a building or its enclosure, serious
damage is possible given time.
It is not often appreciated how little leakage is required to
cause serious problems. For example, a leaky valve or window
that leaks one drop of water per hour onto a drywall ceiling
below is likely to cause rapid and potentially serious mold
growth. Further, liquid water from these sources can quickly
reach catastrophic proportions. Rain leaks or plumbing pipe
failures can result in hundreds of gallons of water being dis-
charged into a building.
Water vapor can be almost as problematic as direct liquid
water sources, although the magnitude of the moisture involved
is typically much less. Water vapor condensation on
uninsulated chilled water or domestic cold water pipe is a
relatively common problem, as is condensation hidden within
walls and roofs or running down windows. The water vapor
from the exterior enters the building both through intentional
supply air ventilation and unintentional air leaks through the
building enclosure and ducts.
In many types of buildings, a significant amount of mois-
ture can be released or generated by occupants, their activi-
ties, and processes. Table 1 lists different internal moisture
sources and their significance. This source of vapor is highly
variable. The amount of water provided by a well used heated
indoor pool or papermaking process is obviously very signifi-
cant whereas the moisture given off by a two people in a large
home is not. Design engineers should investigate specific pro-
cesses in some detail to allow the prediction of average and
peak vapor production.
Soil can be a significant source of moisture near the base-
ment, foundation, and the first floor of buildings. Soil is a
large source of moisture in both liquid and vapor forms. Liq-
uid water, draining directly from the surface or from the water
table tends to penetrate through cracks, holes and other unin-
tentional openings. The liquid water stored within the soil
matrix will wick through the soil and porous building materi-
als (concrete, stone, wood, etc.).
Stored liquid water deep below the surface and bound to the
soil also provides a practically inexhaustible supply of water
