This colder, drier supply air results in a drier space at all load
conditions. For example, at the peak dry-bulb condition, the
space relative humidity is 47%, compared to 52% with a more-
traditional supply-air temperature.
Summary
HVAC systems have historically focused on controlling the
space dry-bulb temperature, while space dehumidification was
coincidental. The widely used single-zone, constant-volume
system can be the most problematic when it comes to dehu-
midification, particularly at part-load conditions. VAV systems,
however, generally provide effective, indirect dehumidifica-
tion over a wide range of indoor load conditions.
When properly designed and controlled, the HVAC system
can significantly reduce the moisture content of indoor air. Ana-
lyze system dehumidification performance at both full- and
part-load conditions, and consider the advantages and disad-
vantages of each system enhancement. The enhancements dis-
cussed in this article are detailed further in Reference 3. The
right choice for a given project depends on the climate, build-
ing use, available budget, and operating cost goals.
Bibliography
1. ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62.1–2001, Ventilation for Acceptable
Indoor Air Quality.
2. ANSI/ASHRAE/IESNA Standard 90.1–2001, Energy Standard
for Buildings Except Low-Rise Residential Buildings.
3. Murphy, J. 2002. Dehumidification in HVAC Systems, applica-
tions engineering manual, SYS-APM004-EN, Trane.
4. Stanke, D., et al. 1998. Managing Building Moisture, applications
engineering manual, SYS-AM-15, Trane.
5. Stanke, D. and B. Bradley. 2000. “Dehumidify with constant-
volume systems.” Engineers Newsletter, 29(4) Trane.
6. Stanke, D. and B. Bradley. 2001. “Dedicated ventilation systems.”
Engineers Newsletter, 30(3) Trane.
7. U.S. Environmental Protection Agency. 2001. Mold Remediation
in Schools and Commercial Buildings, EPA 402-K-01-001, March
(www.epa.gov/iaq/molds).
40
March 2002|ASHRAE Journal
he roof often is the most ignored equipment location in a refriger-
ated facility. Access is typically difficult so employees avoid going up
to the roof any more than necessary. The difficult access accounts for
the minimal visitation by supervisors and corporate engineers. Reports
of “what’s happening up there?” are secondhand, given by those who
occasionally climb up as part of their routine duties.
Accessing Rooftop
Refrigeration Systems
About the Author
By Rudy Stegmann, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Rudy Stegmann, P.E., is president of The En-
thalpy Exchange, Williamsburg, Va.
What makes access difficult? Most
plants have flat roofs about 35 ft (11 m)
high with access provided by a ladder
clamped within a few inches of an ex-
ternal wall. Or a similar ladder is located
within a small closet inside the build-
ing, which requires lifting a heavy trap-
door to access the roof.
Access is hampered by the array
of piping (refrigerant, water, steam,
process fluids and electrical), especially
on older facilities that seem to have
grown without direction or control.
The roof is an obstacle course—climb-
ing over some pipes, crawling under
others—by the time you get where
you are going, you have forgotten why
you came!
Restricted access can result in in-
creased risk of accident every time em-
ployees are on the roof. Emergency
response teams suited with self-con-
tained breathing apparatus may not be
able to get on the roof or move freely
from place to place, which places the
entire plant at risk.
Cause for Concern
Unfortunately, difficult access sup-
ports a minimum maintenance ap-
proach. Reports of problems can go
unheeded until the reporting simply
ceases. Management assumes all is well.
Why be concerned about the roof? Only
a few pipes are up there. What can hap-
pen to them anyway—they’re steel?
Management may not realize that
some of the pipes and valves may be
severely rusted, insulation integrity may
have been breached and insulated pipes
may be encased in tubes of ice. Some
valves may not operate. The stems have
rusted to the bonnets and cannot be
turned without breaking the valve.
Long runs of hot gas lines may have be-
gun to sag sufficiently to harbor large
slugs of liquid refrigerant, which are
ready to slam into coils at defrost. Pipes
shake and rattle. A solenoid valve re-
ceives its power from a loose junction
box that dangles from its wires, some of
which are rubbing at the sharp edge of
the broken conduit fitting.
This could be a set up for disaster! Or,
at best, a major problem if ignored.
Preventing Problems
Roof Access
Roof Access
Roof Access
Roof Access
Roof Access
Reasonable access to the roof is a good
starting place. When people can get to
the roof easily, they will be likely to visit
more often. More people inspecting the
equipment more frequently will result in
finding and correcting minor problems
before they can cause a tragic incident.
Roof access should adhere to the appli-
cable building, electrical, refrigeration
and other suitable codes, as well as indus-
try guidelines of acceptable practice.
ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 15-1994, Safety
Code for Mechanical Refrigeration, and
the IIAR-2 Standard for Equipment, De-
sign and Installation of Ammonia Me-
chanical Refrigerating Systems, are the
primary sources. IIAR also publishes
guidelines of acceptable practice that are
reasonable to follow to minimize risk.
Some important access items are:
• Replace the vertical ladder (a mini-
mum requirement) with a fire-rated stair-
case. This makes it easier to access the
roof, and facilitates carrying tools and re-
placement parts up and scrap parts down.
Often, dead motors, pumps and control
parts remain scattered about long after
T
ASHRAE Journal|March 2002
41
Rooftops
removal. Good housekeeping is essential to safety. No one needs
to trip on a dead motor.
• It is equally important to have an alternate route of escape in
an emergency. An ammonia release may blow towards the stair-
case, or a building fire may engulf the stairs. What a relief to
know that there is another way down—besides jumping!
• Some older facilities have several levels of roofs. It is some-
times common to use stacked boxes, old dilapidated wood lad-
ders or even old wood pallets leaning at an angle as a ladder.
Any rise above 18 in. (457 mm) needs permanently installed
steps to prevent accidents.
Most refrigeration plants have a maze of all sorts of piping, so
getting from place to place is not always easy. Some piping lies
close to the roofline. Some pipes are elevated several feet. When
direct access to a specific valve station is impaired, catwalks
and bridges must be used.
Lighting
Lighting
Lighting
Lighting
Lighting
The next consideration is adequate lighting. Some plants have
virtually no roof lighting while others do not provide suitable
light for all service areas. The locations of catwalks are great
indicators of which areas need lighting.
Lighting is extremely important for the safety of personnel on
the roof. There will be fewer tripping accidents and less chance
of getting too near the edge.
Railings
Railings
Railings
Railings
Railings
In areas where the serviceable equipment is close to the roof’s
edge, protective railings or fences are required. The entire
roofline does not need to be railed, only areas where personnel
will service equipment and along catwalks at the piping bridges.
Rooftop Mechanical Integrity
Roof access, lighting and rails permit control of the integrity
of the equipment on the roof. Now, it is easy to access the roof
and more people can observe the conditions and plan corrective
activities. The following sections discuss the major areas of con-
cern on the roof.
Piping Systems
Piping Systems
Piping Systems
Piping Systems
Piping Systems
The condition of piping on the roof varies significantly with
the geographical location of the building. It is necessary to
consider the effects of local pollutants in the air (which may
come from adjacent plants or even from the refrigerated facil-
ity). Each location has its own set of contaminating factors that
will result in the rusting of unprotected steel piping.
All piping should be adequately painted to prevent rusting.
This is especially necessary when repairs or additions are made
to a piping system. Occasionally, new welds, flanges and piping
sections are left unpainted and unprotected. In coastal areas,
these repairs can begin to rust within a week. Properly protected,
it will be many years before any rust becomes evident.
Piping support brackets, especially on older buildings, tend
to rust and collapse causing the piping to sag. Support brackets
need to be kept in good condition and replaced when showing
signs of deterioration. Sagging pipes can fill with liquid refrig-
erant and provide dangerous slugs of liquid refrigerant to the
system. Unsupported pipes also are subject to vibration and
possible breakage. Maintaining a proper support system is an
excellent way to reduce the risk of a major ammonia release.
Regular visual inspection of the piping system should be
made and any questionable items should be corrected. A con-
tractor with test equipment should evaluate sections with seri-
ous rust or corrosion to indicate whether the pipe needs to be
replaced.
Piping should be adequately identified with acceptable mark-
ers. The markers ahould identify the condition of the refrigerant
within the pipe and the direction of flow. Where there are several
temperature levels, identify the temperatures of the liquid and
return lines. Several manufacturers make pipe markers for am-
monia refrigeration.
Insulation
Insulation
Insulation
Insulation
Insulation
Piping that carries refrigerant at temperatures below freezing
is insulated to prevent infiltration of heat into the refrigerant,
which would result in a loss of system capacity. The insulated
pipe has a protective covering typically of aluminum or a vinyl
material. The insulation system must have an uninterrupted va-
por barrier over the insulation that prevents water vapor or rain
from getting into the insulation. Both of these functions of insu-
lation are absolutely required to ensure that minimal heat infil-
tration into the cold refrigerant piping occurs (insulation dry)
and that moisture from all sources is prevented from entering
the insulated system to avoid insulation degradation and pipe
corrosion.
Many insulation systems terminate the insulation at control
valves, leaving the control valve uninsulated. This permits easy
service of the valve. How the insulation is terminated is ex-
tremely important. The terminated end must be sealed with a
suitable mastic vapor barrier to continue the integrity of the
vapor barrier system. Where there is no vapor barrier, moisture
can enter the insulation and work its way along the pipeline.
This destroys the effect of the insulation. Wet insulation is no
insulation! Make certain that the termination of insulation is
always sealed with a vapor barrier.
Visual inspection of insulated piping can indicate the overall
condition of the insulated system. If the bottom of the pipe is
cold and wet or icy, the vapor barrier has been violated and
moisture is in the insulation. Piping underneath wet insulation
may be seriously corroded to the point of failure, which cannot
be detected until the insulation is removed.
Such piping must have the insulation removed. The pipe must
be inspected to determine whether corrosion damage exists that
is sufficient to warrant replacement of the pipe. Re-insulation of
the existing piping or of new piping involves cleaning and
painting the pipe and installing the insulation system accord-
42
March 2002|ASHRAE Journal
ing to manufacturer’s requirements. Insulation should only be
applied to clean, painted, dry piping.
Relief V
Relief V
Relief V
Relief V
Relief Valve V
alve V
alve V
alve V
alve Venting
enting
enting
enting
enting
Most of the relief valves from the refrigeration system are
vented on the roof area. The codes require that the vents be
located at least 12 ft (3.65 m) above the adjacent rooftop. The
codes also stipulate that the outlet of the vent should not be
directed toward any ventilation opening of the building or to-
ward any location where operators may be stationed.
Roofs should be inspected to ensure that the requirements are
met. Many relief valves are vented only a few feet above the
roofline and some can be found aimed at locations where a
service technician may be working.
Another misapplication of the relief valve vents is on indi-
vidual vent lines that extend 12 ft (3.65 m) or more above the
roof. These vent lines often are not
braced and may sway in the breeze, piv-
oting from the location where the safety
relief valve is connected to the vessel it
protects. This arrangement also is an
accident waiting to happen! Failure will
not occur in the vent pipe but at the
vessel connection, which may result in
a large ammonia release.
Valve Stations
alve Stations
alve Stations
alve Stations
alve Stations
Valve stations are areas on the roof
where a group of valves are associated
with a particular evaporator or set of
evaporators. These occur outside pent-
houses and at locations where refriger-
ant lines enter the building feeding
evaporator coils. The refrigerant lines
at these locations are typically a liquid
supply line, a suction return line and a
high pressure hot gas defrost line.
These valve stations are located on
the roof for two primary purposes. One
is to provide easier access to the valves
since most coils are located just below
the roof, inside the cold room. The sec-
ond is to minimize any ammonia leak-
age into the refrigerated area when
servicing the valves.
The types of valves at the valve sta-
tions are usually shut-off valves, strain-
ers, solenoid valves, hand-expansion
valves, check valves, pressure regula-
tors and gauge valves. Each valve
station’s connection to a particular
evaporator or set of evaporators needs
to be identified.
As previously mentioned, insulation
on the cold liquid and suction lines typi-
cally terminates at the valves. The
valves, although painted by the manu-
facturer when new, are often ignored
when painting is done on the piping
system. Sooner or later, they begin to
rust. The same is true for the flanges,
Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
ASHRAE Journal|March 2002
43
Rooftops
which are welded to the piping to support the control valves.
These are often unpainted or the welding burns off any paint
that may be on them. It is legal to paint a manufacturer’s valve
with your standard color.
Solenoid valves are an important part of the control at
these stations. Make certain that the solenoid valve
functions and that wiring to the solenoids is intact. Junction
boxes with missing covers and junction boxes hanging on
the conduit without being permanently attached are prob-
lems to be corrected.
Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative Condensers
Evaporative condensers are the largest pieces of equipment
on the roof and require a good bit of attention and service to
maintain them properly. The evaporative condenser operates by
spraying water over the condenser tubes while, at the same time,
blowing air over the coils to evaporate some of the water to
condense the refrigerant. The water not evaporated returns to a
sump to be pumped through the spray nozzles again. This open
type system has several control parameters that must be moni-
tored and addressed.
The condition of the water must be controlled so that
any biologic growth is suppressed. This control is designed
to kill virtually all bacteria, slimes and mold. The tempera-
ture of the water in the sump is typically 80°F to 85°F
(27°C to 29°C), which is ideal growth temperature for bacte-
ria, slimes and mold. It is impossible to eliminate all bacteria
in an open system. However, it can be controlled at limits
below which colonization can occur. Slime and mold
provide the food for bacteria. Elimination of these helps mini-
mize bacterial growth.
One of the worst offenders is the Legionella bacterium, which
is found in all water supplies. Uncontrolled growth can occur
within the warm water of the condenser. Breathing the mist from
the condenser is one way Legionnaire’s disease is contracted.
Biological growth is controlled in almost all systems by us-
ing chemicals which, when added to the water, maintain the
count of bacteria below a safe quantity. An alternate method is
the use of ozone (produced at the jobsite) that is injected into
the water. Both methods are effective in killing virtually all
bacteria and virus contamination when properly applied. Un-
fortunately, there are systems that do not employ any biological
control. These systems may be harboring uncontrolled harmful
bacteria. Employees at these locations are at risk of contracting
a serious disease from inhalation of mist from the spray towers.
Control of condenser tube scaling is also important. Scaling
results from the plating of minerals in the water onto the galva-
nized steel tubes. With chemically treated systems the water is
softened and scaling is minimized. The ozone system is used
in conjunction with a magnetic water conditioner that ionizes
the water to prevent scaling. A benefit of the ozone system is
that the rate of bleed-off water can be significantly reduced.
Bleed-off is a fixed quantity of water that is continually drained
from the sump to keep the mineral content of the water stabi-
lized at a desired quantity. This process counteracts the miner-
als introduced with the makeup, which is water added to the
sump to replace the evaporated water. Evaporation of water
provides the heat removal necessary to condense the refriger-
ant vapor to a liquid.
Open evaporative condenser systems also are excellent air
washers. Any contaminants are drawn through the system,
washed out of the air and become part of the water sump. This
includes chemicals, dust, pollen and airborne seeds. It is neces-
sary to regularly inspect and clean the water distribution system
to ensure that the trays and nozzles are not blocked. Blockage
prevents full wetting of the coils and reduces the capacity of the
condenser, thereby increasing the condensing pressure.
Energy Savings and Capacity Improvement
Until now, every improvement suggested involved spend-
ing money. The reduced risk of ammonia release, extended
equipment life and improved employee safety are nebulous
quantities that have a different value for various organiza-
tions. However, proper maintenance of the rooftop equip-
ment can result in a more definite savings analysis, especially
as associated with the evaporative condensers and degraded
pipe insulation.
Energy savings and capacity improvement can be achieved
by cleaning and maintaining the condensers and replacing de-
graded (failed) insulation systems.
Most industrial ammonia refrigeration systems operate con-
tinuously year-round, with the internal product cooling load
varying little throughout the year. Operating hours can reach
8,000 hours or more per year. Let’s assume a 1,000-ton (3517
kW) system operating at various temperature ranges, centering
at –40°F, –20°F and 20°F (–40°C, –29°C and –6°C) evaporating
conditions. Also, assume that this system operates fully loaded
for 4,000 hours each year.
The condenser savings include reducing electrical power and
improving compressor capacity. Less power is needed to drive
the compressors because maintained condensers provide lower
condensing pressures and thus, less compressor power. There
will also be an improvement in compressor capacity (more tons
refrigeration) at the reduced condensing pressure. Both factors
lead to an optimization of the equipment, and the most refrig-
eration for the dollar spent.
Bleed-off water quantity is a necessity. However, depending on
the specific mineral content of the water, and the type of water
treatment, significant reduction in the bleed rate can be tolerated,
the cost of the quantity of bleed water adds up. This is based on
water cost plus appropriate sewerage charges, if applicable.
Liquid Line Insulation
Liquid Line Insulation
Liquid Line Insulation
Liquid Line Insulation
Liquid Line Insulation
Failed insulation, as evidenced by a coating of ice, is a source
of unnecessary loss of system capacity as well as an increase in
wasted power.
44
March 2002|ASHRAE Journal
Let’s assume a 2 in. (50 mm) liquid line for –20°F and –40°F
(–29°C and –40°C) systems. Line length to the valve stations is
600 ft (182 m). The insulation is 2 in. (50 mm) thick and water
saturated. The outer jacket is about 6.5 in. (165 mm) diameter
and fully coated with ice. There will be a capacity loss (refriger-
ant heat gain) due to both air convection and radiation. This loss
is estimated at 1.5 tons/hundred feet of line. The total loss in
booster capacity (at –40°F, –20°F [–40°C, –29°C) will be 9 tons
(32 kW). The booster capacity loss means that part of the power
input for the booster and the high stage are wasted by trying to
cool the roof.
The estimated power for the booster is 1 hp/ton and for the
high stage is 1.4 hp/ton. Total power will then be 2.4 hp/ton. The
total wasted horsepower for 9 tons (32 kW) is 24 hp (18 kW).
@$0.06/kWh = $1.07/hour
@ 4,000 hrs = $4,297 loss per year
When the insulation is corrected, there will be a capacity gain
of 9 tons (32 kW) in the low temperature evaporators and a
saving in previously wasted horsepower of $4,297/4,000 hours.
Conclusion
Go up to the roof and look around. It is easy to find things that
are shaking, making funny noises, rusting, iced over or dripping
with green slime, for example. You may not know how to fix
them but you know what is obviously broken. Call attention to
the problems and get them fixed. It will save money, reduce the
risk of accidents and of ammonia release.

ASHRAE Journal|April 2002
37
Lake-Source
Cooling
About the Authors
T
Tim Peer, P.E., is heat exchange facility project
engineer at the Utilities Department, Cornell Uni-
versity, Ithaca, N.Y.
W. S. (Lanny) Joyce, P.E., is lake-source cooling
project manager at the Utilities Department,
Cornell University, Ithaca, N.Y.
he Lake-Source Cooling (LSC) Project at Cornell University uses
the deep, cold waters of nearby Cayuga Lake, as a non-contact
cooling source for the campus chilled water system. It began providing
16,000 tons (56 300 kWth) of cooling in July 2000 with an 86% reduc-
tion in energy use versus conventional cooling alternatives. Cornell’s
deep-water-source cooling project is the first to use a deep inland lake’s
renewable cold water. (All heat gained each year is fully released to the
environment by midwinter. LSC’s contribution is equivalent to an addi-
tional two hours of sunlight each year.) Two technically challenging is-
sues of this project were heat exchanger selection and distribution
system hydronics.
Campus District Cooling System
Most research universities boast physi-
cal plants large enough to serve a small
city. Cornell’s nearly 40-year-old central
chilled water system has more than 75
buildings connected to a looped distri-
bution-piping network of more than 10
miles (16.1 km). Three central plants with
eight chillers and an aboveground 4.4-
million-gallon (16.7 × 106 L) stratified
chilled water thermal storage tank1 pro-
vided cooling before LSC. Load growth,
phase out of CFC refrigerants, aging
equipment and rising energy costs all
contributed to the need for major change.
Six of the eight chillers could not be eco-
nomically retrofitted to new refrigerants,
requiring their replacement before 2005.
Lake-Source Cooling
LSC is designed to provide more than
20,000 tons (70 400 kWth) of peak cool-
ing capacity, circulating up to 32,000
gpm (2020 L/s). It has reduced chiller
energy use by 86% with associated re-
ductions in emissions of pollutants pro-
duced in the generation of electricity in
regional power plants. LSC also offers a
75- to 100-year system life, versus 30 to
40 years typical for chillers.
The project begins physically with a
screened intake structure located in a
water depth of 250 ft (76.2 m) near the
southern end of Cayuga Lake, which is
the second largest of the Finger Lakes in
New York. This glacial lake is roughly
38-miles (61 km) long, 1 mile (1.6 km)
wide, up to 425 ft (130 m) deep with 2.5
trillion gallons (9.48 × 1012 L) volume.
A variable flow of up to 32,000 gpm
(2020 L/s) of 39°F (4°C) lake water
(which has the highest density for fresh
water) enters the intake and travels
through nearly 2 miles (3.22 km) of 63
in. (1.6 m) high-density polyethylene
(HDPE) plastic pipe to arrive at a wet well
in the heat exchange facility. In the plant,
the water is pumped out of the wet well
by up to three 350 hp (261 kWe) variable
speed vertical turbine pumps, and
pushed through up to seven plate and
frame heat exchangers piped in parallel.
The lake water then returns to the lake
through a 100 ft (30.5 m) diffuser sec-
tion, which ends 500 ft (152 m) off shore
in about 14 ft (4.27 m) of water depth.
Chilled water flows from campus dis-
tribution piping through five intercon-
nection points that combine into 42 in.
(1.07 m) of coated welded steel transmis-
sion piping that extends 2.5 miles (4.02
km) to the lakeshore heat exchange fa-
cility. The closed loop of campus water
is pulled through the heat exchangers and
then pushed by the pumps back to cam-
pus to minimize operating pressures in
the plant. The five 600 hp (447 kWe) vari-
able speed centrifugal pumps and trans-
mission piping are designed for high
static pressure due to the nearly 600 ft
(183 m) height between the top of cam-
pus loads and the plant.
System Performance
The system started operation in July
By Tim Peer, P.E., Associate Member ASHRAE, and
W. S. (Lanny) Joyce, P.E., Member ASHRAE
Lake-Source
Cooling
Exterior view of the completed heat exchange facility show-
ing many of the architectural features.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April, 2002. © Copyright 2002 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
38
April 2002|ASHRAE Journal
2000. By the end of the month, it was handling all campus-
cooling load with a summer peak that year of 15,500 tons (54
600 kWth). The peak sustained demand in 2001 was more than
16,000 tons (56 300 kWth). Since startup, LSC has been operat-
ing at an annual average of 0.10 kW/ton (0.028 kWe/kWth).
With a typical annual production of more than 30 million ton-
hours (106 × 106 kWth), LSC will save more than 22 million
kWh of electricity.
The plant is fully automated and integrated into the overall
utilities control system for monitoring by operators 3 miles
(4.83 km) distant from the LSC plant. A fiber-optic link allows
the operators to see plant functions instantly. Today, the cam-
pus return temperature allows 18,000 tons (63 400 kWth) of
peak heat rejection, and as the campus return temperature rises,
the ultimate capacity will be 20,000 tons (70 400 kWth).
Heat Exchanger Design and Selection
As stated earlier, heat is transferred from the chilled water
system to the lake water system through seven plate and frame
heat exchangers. The 300 psi (2069 kPa) rated exchangers are
located on the suction side of the chilled water pumps where
the normal operating pressure is typically 200 to 250 psi (1380
to 1720 kPa). The heat exchangers are designed to provide
42°F (5.6°C) chilled water at a 3°F (1.7°C) approach. Campus
loads are designed as primary-secondary variable flow mixing
loops providing 47°F (8.3°C) chilled water in the summer and
50°F (10°C) during the winter to the buildings.
Total load on the plant includes the buildings and heat gain
to the 5 miles (8.05 km) of transmission piping that accounts
for 800 tons (2820 kWth) at peak. With peak flow and all but
lake return temperature fixed, surface area was determined by
balancing heat transfer coefficient and water pressure drop.
Fouling factor was included by adding one spare unit.
All vendors were requested to supply a base bid and two
alternates (less quantity of exchangers) to allow optimization
based on guaranteed vendor data during the review process.
The optimization process included evaluation of heat transfer
coefficient versus pressure drop. More surface area increases
first cost but decreases operating costs. The project team de-
veloped a model to “run” the heat exchangers with the ex-
pected annual load profile and create a “cost of ownership.”
Final selection was made on present value.
The present value of reducing pressure drop across the plate
heat exchangers by 1 psi (6.9 kPa) was estimated for a 10-year
period. Because of the large savings potential, a design 10 psi
(69 kPa) pressure drop across the PHE was selected for the base
bids. During the optimization process, the vendor guaranteed
data and costs drove the selection to a 14 psi (96.5 kPa) pres-
sure drop, increasing available thermal capacity for the few
short hours each year the plant will run at peak flows, with
minimal extra operating costs during off-peak conditions.
Campus Distribution Study
The previous Cornell system had three chilled water plants
located on the periphery of the campus looped distribution
system. The LSC system feeds directly into the center of the
existing system, significantly changing the system hydrau-
lics. A campus distribution study using a flow analysis pro-
gram determined the optimal tie-in of the LSC transmission
pipeline, the sections of the distribution system requiring up-
grading, and the required LSC chilled water pump head. The
following constraints were used when performing the hydrau-
lic analysis:
• The minimum supply and return differential pressure at
any building connection would be 5 psid (34.5 kPa).
• The maximum supply and return differential pressure at
any building connection would be 30 psid (207 kPa).
• The maximum supply pressure would not exceed 140 psig
(965 kPa).
• Fluid velocities would not exceed 10 ft/s (3.0 m/s).
• The return system pressure at the makeup point would be
60 psig (414 kPa).
Base demands and system configurations were determined
and modeled for 2000, 2015 and 2029. Modifications were
made to the base models to optimize the tie-ins and determine
upgrades to the existing distribution system.
Interior view of the completed heat exchange facility.
Schematic of the lake-source cooling process.
ASHRAE Journal|April 2002
39
Technology Award
Booster Pump Station
A challenge arose due to a 3,000 ft (914 m) radial spur feed-
ing the east side of campus. The minimum differential pressure
requirements could not be met using pumps at LSC without
exceeding the maximum differential pressure requirements in
the core campus.
A series pump booster station was added, reducing core cam-
pus pressures and overall pumping horsepower by 350 hp (261
kWe), or more than 10%. High system flow turndown reduces
the pump’s duty to less than 500 hours per year.
The pump is a single-stage, horizontal split-case, double-
suction pump rated for 10,000 gpm (631 L/s) at a head of 47 ft
(140 Pa). The high-flow, low-head condition required a cre-
ative pump and motor selection to purchase standard prod-
ucts. A 900 rpm, 120 hp (89.5 kWe) pump design point is met
through the use of a 1200 rpm, 200 hp (149 kWe) motor on a
variable speed drive. The drive is configured to operate at a
maximum speed of 900 rpm matching the rated speed of the
pump. The pump impeller is uncut to provide the greatest effi-
ciency.
The flow rate of the pump is controlled by a variable fre-
quency drive that regulates the speed according to the differ-
ential pressure at the most remote load. The pump operates
continuously throughout the year (much of it at minimum
speed), eliminating the need to provide space heating and en-
suring proper bearing lubrication.
See www.utilities.cornell.edu /lsc for more information.
References
1. Bahnfleth, W.P. and W.S. Joyce. 1995. “Stratified storage eco-
nomically increases capacity and efficiency of campus chilled water
system.” ASHRAE Journal 37(3):46–49.

ASHRAE Journal|April 2002
1
About the Author
his article addresses maintaining constant minimum outside air in
variable air volume (VAV) systems as supply air quantity changes.
Figure 1 shows a typical outside air (OA) intake system and lists some
factors that affect flow through the OA intake. The outside air mini-
mum ventilation control must be coordinated with those other fac-
tors.1,2,3,4,5,6 A discussion of how to achieve that coordination is re-
served for another article. This article focuses on the minimum out-
side air control.
Mechanical Configurations
Air-handling unit configuration affects
the minimum outside air control method
selection. The many equipment varia-
tions include dedicated minimum out-
side air dampers, injection fans, separate
DDC outputs with individual actuators
for each damper, and variations in fan
locations.
Figures 2 and 3 show common damper
arrangements. Nearly infinite possible
geometric configurations exist, so no
single method of control is always best.
The handful of control methods dis-
cussed in this article can be applied suc-
cessfully in most cases.
Figure 2A is typical of most factory-
built air-handling units and many built-
up systems. The single outside air damper
meets the minimum outside air ventila-
tion requirement and functions as an
outside air economizer. Figure 2B shows
separate minimum outside air and econo-
mizer dampers.
Figure 3A is similar to Figure 2B ex-
cept that multisection dampers are re-
quired due to the large system size.
Damper D1 typically would be open
whenever the fan is on. It is sized using
orifice ratios and mixed air plenum pres-
sure at full supply design. Damper D2
modulates to achieve constant minimum
outside air as the supply fan flow varies.
To size these dampers, the designer must
know the design mixed air plenum pres-
sures and estimate the loss coefficients
and pressure losses in all the duct paths.
The pressure loss through a damper
treated as an orifice is unclear. However,
for practical purposes the values in Table
1 will work using ∆Pt = C × Pv.

When sizing the damper by flow coeffi-
cient, apply a safety factor so the bal-
ancer can partially close the blades to
adjust for the real mixed air pressure.
Control Methods
There are six methods commonly used
to control the quantity of ventilation air
Minimum Outside Air
Damper Control
By Larry Felker, Associate Member ASHRAE
Many systems set the minimum out-
side airflow with the system operating at
maximum (design) supply airflow. As the
supply airflow decreases, the amount of
outside air decreases proportionately.
Proponents of this approach suggest a
direct relationship exists between system
cooling load and the number of occu-
pants. Under that theory, the amount of
outside air required decreases as the load
(and system airflow) decreases. The
theory may be valid for some buildings
some of the time, but it is not true for all
buildings all of the time.
Another method is to set the minimum
outside air quantity at system minimum
supply airflow. This approach maintains
the desired minimum outside air quan-
tity but wastes energy because excess
outside air comes in when the system
operates at more than minimum supply
airflow.
This article describes alternatives to
those methods.
Larry Felker is technical operations manager
for Belimo Aircontrols (USA), Western Region,
Sparks, N.V.
T
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April, 2002. © Copyright 2002 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in
paper form without permission of ASHRAE.
2
April 2002|ASHRAE Journal
entering a building:
1. Flow monitoring using hot wire anemometers, thermistor
sensors, velocity (total less static) pressure sensing, or vortex
shedding.
2. Static pressure differential control across fixed duct ele-
ments or with respect to outside air pressure.
3. Temperature comparison of outside air, return air, and
mixed air to determine the ratios.
4. Use of commissioning data to relate system flow at vari-
ous fan-operating points with OA damper actuator signal posi-
tion.
5. Measuring supply airflow and return airflow and assum-
ing that the difference is the amount of OA. This method is not
recommended and is not discussed in this article. Often, mea-
surement error and duct leakage can exceed the minimum quan-
tity. Duct leakage varies with supply in VAV.7
6. CO2 surrogate sensing, which is not discussed here.
Each method has its advantages and problems. Multiple
point measurement is usually required, and the regularity of
the flow profile is critical. Space constraints are a constant
difficulty. Maintenance is too
often ignored, and filtration
before the stations may be re-
quired.
Flow Monitoring
Outside air typically enters
the mixed air plenum via a
duct to outdoors. One control
strategy to maintain a con-
stant minimum OA quantity
is to measure the amount of
ventilation air and vary the
pressure in the air-handling
unit mixing box to maintain the desired minimum outside
airflow. Direct measurement is the preferred method of control.
When measuring outside airflow, the velocity must be high
enough for sensor accuracy. Figure 3B shows a transition to
keep the velocity through the louver low (to avoid entraining
rain and snow) while keeping it high enough to get useful
sensor readings for flow monitoring.
The requirement for a regular flow profile to obtain accurate
measurement is often overlooked. In Figure 4A, the monitor is
only able to achieve ±10% accuracy. In Figure 4B, the mea-
surement is inaccurate because the sensor is influenced by
changes in the flow profile as the damper rotates. Spacing of
2D before a station and 1D behind it is normally sufficient.
Differential Static Pressure Control
Differential static pressure involves maintaining a constant
pressure in the mixed air plenum or other reference point in the
duct.8,9,10 With constant pressure in the mixed air plenum and a
fixed minimum damper, the minimum OA quantity remains
constant as the supply air fan modulates. Static pressure sen-
sors are simple, reliable, and require little space. To implement
this control, the balancing contractor provides the data and
then the engineer and control contractor need to calculate the
setpoint.
Constant Mixed Air Plenum Pressure
Constant Mixed Air Plenum Pressure
Constant Mixed Air Plenum Pressure
Constant Mixed Air Plenum Pressure
Constant Mixed Air Plenum Pressure
In one differential pressure control method, a fixed mixed
air plenum pressure is maintained by modulating the return
damper as shown in Figure 5. Two damper arrangements are
possible: maximum and minimum dampers or a single damper
used for both ventilation and economizer. The two-damper
approach works best. P1 is mounted outside the building and
must be dampened to avoid the wind effects.
The fan and ductwork are sized so the supply air fan pro-
duces a mixed air plenum pressure that is sufficiently negative
to bring in the desired minimum OA quantity at minimum
supply airflow. At minimum supply airflow, the return air damper
might close to 25% open. As the supply air quantity increases,
the return air damper modulates open to keep the mixed air
plenum pressure constant as
the return airflow increases.
Wind effects on the OA and
exhaust air louvers can cause
inaccuracies if the mixed air
plenum pressure is referenced
to outdoors. Therefore, this
method works better if a pro-
tected chase or duct brings the
OA into the air-handling unit.
Filter loss can vary signifi-
cantly, so filters should not be
installed before the static
pressure sensor in this design.
The minimum OA damper is usually two-position. Its open
position is fixed mechanically during commissioning. The
balancing contractor sets the minimum OA damper at system
design flow. Depending on space pressure and fan capacity
control, the return air damper might be full open or partially
throttled. The pressure in the mixed air during this set-up pro-
cedure must be measured and given to the control contractor.
The return air damper is modulated to maintain this pressure.
Separate analog output to each actuator is required.
Modulation for mixed air-temperature control in the econo-
mizer cycle occurs as in other systems. A minimum must be
maintained, but the damper can open further if necessary to
maintain the desired mixed air temperature (MAT). The energy
penalty for the extra pressure across the return air damper should
be considered when evaluating this scheme.
Serious analysis is necessary during design for the mechani-
cal system to perform as required. The maintenance of all pres-
sures, linearization of damper response, and accuracy of control
are not easy to achieve. Damper loss coefficients need to be

In Figure 5, the minimum OA damper path serves as a fixed
orifice. The differential pressure across it is held constant by
modulating the return air damper. When the minimum OA path
is fixed, its C is constant. Since ∆Pt = C × Pv, holding ∆Pt
constant makes the velocity through the damper and the re-
sulting velocity pressure also constant.

0.27 in. w.g. (67 Pa) pressure drop from outdoors into the mixed

path are trivial at the low air velocity at minimum flow. This
pressure drop is achieved by throttling the damper during bal-

April 2002|ASHRAE Journal
about 0.5, so the minimum damper face area will be about
2/0.5 = 4 ft2 (0.37 m2) (obtained from damper manufacturer
charts). Free area ratio varies considerably by damper manu-
facturer, so this calculation must be based on the damper actu-
ally installed.
If the air-handling unit is 5 ft wide × 5 ft high (1.5 × 1.5 m),
a 10 in. × 60 in. (25 cm × 152 cm) damper will likely be se-
lected (4 ft2 / 5 ft × 12 in. per ft = 9.6 in.). A long, low damper
like this experiences pressure loss higher than the free area
ratio alone indicates. The dominance of the blade stop creates
losses like a sharp-edged orifice. The safety factor covers this
problem.
If the damper were mounted in a side-entrance mixing box,
the blade stop would be a minor component, while the side
seal would be more in proportion. The side-seal portion creates
a smooth shape that has less loss than the blade stop. This
example shows there is some art to “damper as orifice” sizing.
The differential pressure sensor should be selected for a mid-
point pressure near the control point (0.27 in. w.g. [67 Pa] in
this example). A 0 to 1 in. w.g. (249 Pa) sensor likely will be the
closest that is commercially available. A 0 to 0.5 in. w.g. (125
Pa) would be more accurate, but unless the designer is positive
that the mixed air plenum will not be more negative than 0.4
in. w.g. (100 Pa), the higher range should be specified.
The instantaneous accuracy of the system will likely be
within 10% of design. Sensors are realistically within 5% of
specification. Turbulence, logic averaging, actuator deadband
and time delays are all part of inaccuracy. Over a 10-minute
period, the accuracy should be near design.
When transferring control from minimum OA to economizer
mode, the control sequence should recognize the need to main-
tain the minimum OA quantity in the economizer mode. The
economizer dampers are probably non-linear. Some calcula-
tion to blank off a portion of the damper is required. This
makes the mixed air plenum pressure more negative. Several
trials are necessary to find the correct size for all the elements.13
This is a complex method and installation variations can
upset the pressure calculations. However, it can work well as a
retrofit method when space constraints exist.
Modulating Return Air Damper with Flow Monitoring
Modulating Return Air Damper with Flow Monitoring
Modulating Return Air Damper with Flow Monitoring
Modulating Return Air Damper with Flow Monitoring
Modulating Return Air Damper with Flow Monitoring
This approach is a variation of the differential pressure
method and has worked well. A flow monitoring station is
placed in the minimum OA path. More accuracy is available
using monitoring.
Measuring the minimum outside airflow directly has advan-
tages both in control and for record keeping. The return air
damper modulates to control the flow. The minimum OA damper
is a fixed orifice. A separate economizer damper is either single
or multistage.
The method is simple and reliable, and the cost is reason-
able for the safety it provides. The dampers are sized as de-
scribed for the differential pressure method. Sufficient space
for the outside airflow measuring station is not always avail-
able without some strange duct configurations.
Minimum Outside Air Damper as a Fixed Orifice
Minimum Outside Air Damper as a Fixed Orifice
Minimum Outside Air Damper as a Fixed Orifice
Minimum Outside Air Damper as a Fixed Orifice
Minimum Outside Air Damper as a Fixed Orifice
The second type of differential pressure control measures
the pressure drop across the OA damper and holds it constant.
Some designs measure the differential across the damper and
one or two other elements in series with it. If the sensor is
located inside the OA louver to reduce wind effect, the system
is, in effect, the same as holding the mixed air plenum pressure
constant. Figure 5 shows this design concept.
Balancing dampers should be installed. Individual outputs
to each actuator are necessary. Size the minimum OA damper
with a safety factor for field changes and plan to throttle it
partially during setup.
A variation of this scheme modulates the minimum OA
damper and holds the pressure across the balancing damper
constant. P1 is placed between the minimum damper and its
balancing damper. This uses the balancing damper as the fixed
orifice and thus as the measuring orifice.
Mixed Air Temperature Comparison Method
This method is still being used, but it is not recommended
because it will not work reliably over a full range of operating
conditions. When the OA and RA are within 10°F to 20°F (5°C
to 10°C) of one another, depending on the accuracy and time
constant of the sensors, the readings are not trustworthy. For
example, at a return air temperature of 70°F (21°C), an OA
temperature of 62°F (17°C), and 10% OA required, the mixed

air temperature will be 69.2°F (20.6°C). Sensors rarely have
enough accuracy in real-world applications to distinguish those
tiny temperature differences.
The mixed air temperature comparison method relies on the
formula:
%RA
%OA
×
+
×
=
Tra
Toa
Tma
Tma = mixed air temperature
Toa = OA temperature
Tra = return air temperature
%OA = percent OA (expressed as a decimal)
%RA = percent return air (expressed as a decimal)
The formula can be rearranged to calculate the amount of
OA when the other parameters are known. Accuracy degrades
as Toa approaches Tra. Stratification in the mixed air plenum
can affect accuracy. In rooftop equipment, the mixed air sensor
can sometimes read higher than either the OA or return air
sensors depending on location and solar heat gain.
Balancers sometimes use this method to calculate flows when
space does not exist for pitot tube traverses. However, as a
control method it is flawed. Although not suitable for control,
the mixed air temperature comparison method is useful as a
maintenance advisory check of the operation of the static pres-
sure controls when the OA and return air temperatures are at
least 20°F (10°C) apart.
Commissioning Data Method
This method can be complex, labor intensive and requires a
DDC system capable of logic operations. It is sometimes a
good retrofit fix for problems.
For any given system flow, there is some OA damper position
that provides the required OA quantity. In this method, a DDC
system positions the OA damper according to a schedule of
occupancy and total system flow (Figure 6).
Table 2 is an example. Multiple versions of the chart are
required over the range of mixed air plenum pressures. To main-
tain constant minimum OA quantity, the OA damper opens
more as system airflow decreases.
During commissioning, the balancing contractor measures
and records the signals and flows at design, several midpoints,
and minimum fan operating points. The relationship between
the supply fan capacity signal and the OA damper actuator
signal needed for the desired minimum OA quantity is found
and recorded. Therefore, the desired minimum OA quantity
can be maintained by adjusting the signal to the minimum
damper based on the signal to the VAV fan.
One problem with this method is the variation in mixed air
plenum pressure as the filters load with dirt. As the filters load,
the supply fan signal to maintain constant pressure at the sys-
tem static pressure sensor increases. The minimum OA quan-
tity could vary with filter pressure drop. However, if the pressure
drop across the filter is a small portion of the total system loss,
the effect may be inconsequential. In most cases, it needs to be
considered.
Pneumatic actuators with mechanical positioners do not work
well in this application because of inaccurate positioning
(±5%). Process-quality pneumatic actuators with electronic
positioners work better, but they are expensive. As a result,
electronic direct-coupled actuators are preferred. A feedback
signal accurate to within 1% gives the real clamp position.
The inherent positive positioning of this type of actuator is
also accurate to 1%. Since linkages are usually not necessary,
the hysteresis of the linkage and the non-linearity they intro-
duce are also avoided.
This system requires individual analog outputs going to the
minimum OA damper, the economizer damper, the return air
damper, the exhaust air damper, and to each fan. A fan-tracking
or space pressurization sequence may need to be added to the

April 2002|ASHRAE Journal
control. The sequencing of multisection dampers will improve
control. Balancing dampers in the OA, return air, and relief air
ducts may be necessary. A demand-control ventilation sensor
could be incorporated into the design.
If any damper is installed so that it will be difficult to per-
form a valid pitot tube traverse, the use of balancing collars or
other facilitating means is necessary.
Hardware Options
Several hardware options are available for implementing
minimum OA control.
Injection Fan
Injection Fan
Injection Fan
Injection Fan
Injection Fan
Figure 7 shows an injection fan and a finger tube distribu-
tion system for ventilation effectiveness. This method defeats
stratification. An opposed blade damper prevents swirl into
the fan, modulates with greater precision, and requires less
torque. A discharge damper often will allow greater turndown
without entering an unstable portion of the fan curve. A VFD is
preferable to a damper for accuracy.
The OA can be injected directly into the return air duct. A
long duct run outside the air-handling unit could be used to
provide the space to obtain a good flow profile.
The OA quantity does not vary as plenum pressures vary
with wind, stack effect, and VAV operation. The monitor and
VFD on the injection fan ensure a constant minimum quantity.
If the injection fan turns off when changeover to the econo-
mizer occurs, the maximum OA damper must immediately open
enough to keep the OA quantity from falling below that re-
quired for ventilation. This can be achieved in several ways. In
one method, the mixed air temperature may be noted before
economizer operation, then the injection fan is shut down, and
then the economizer damper is opened until the previous mixed
air temperature is reached. The actuator signal is noted at this
point. The damper can open further but is not allowed to close
below the signal just established to achieve the desired mini-
mum OA quantity. Variations in supply air quantity can com-
plicate the matter. Only direct measurement of the OA quantity
gives high confidence. To avoid these problems, the injection
fan probably should stay on, leaving the mixed air tempera-
ture control to modulate the economizer damper as necessary.
One objection to injection fans is a false impression of extra
cost and complexity. An OA injection fan is small and has
technical advantages. Its energy use is much less than the en-
ergy used by the hundreds of fan-powered VAV boxes used
routinely without questioning their energy use.
Modulating Minimum OA Damper
Modulating Minimum OA Damper
Modulating Minimum OA Damper
Modulating Minimum OA Damper
Modulating Minimum OA Damper
New products and methods are entering the market con-
stantly. Flow sensing built into dampers may be the best. Fig-
ure 8 shows a combination damper-flow station. The velocity
pressure is calculated from Pt – Ps and maintained constant by
modulating the minimum OA blades themselves. This has
proven to be simple and reliable in the field. At the low Pv
encountered, this method can read accurately.
VAV Box Controller
VAV Box Controller
VAV Box Controller
VAV Box Controller
VAV Box Controller
Installing a VAV box controller on OA intakes can also keep
minimum air constant. Combination dampers and thermistor

ASHRAE Journal|April 2002
7
Dampers
flow sensors designed to take up minimum space may be
very flexible. This is, in effect, the same method as shown in
Figure 8.
Figure 9 shows a single modulating minimum OA damper
with the minimum duct area reduced to raise the velocity high
enough for good measurement. The losses must be low enough
that the negative pressure in the mixed air plenum can over-
come them. At design flow, the pressures will be more than
enough, but some care is necessary in designing the system so
the pressure is adequate at minimum system flow.
The mixed air plenum pressure is found using the estimated
economizer path losses, return air losses, and fan pressures.
Then the minimum air path is designed. Sizing a complex path
takes a number of trials to find a good setup. The balancing
dampers allow adjustment for contingency and variations in
installation from design geometry.
Conclusion
Given the standards and code requirements for good venti-
lation, care must be applied to designing the outdoor air path.
Since all the air in the building passes through the outdoor air
path, the path must be engineered.
Many good ways exist to control the minimum OA quantity.
No one method is best in all cases. Designers must be innova-
tive to conquer space constraints. They must explain why space
is required to the architect or owner. The mechanical engineer
should be involved on the job site to evaluate the design dur-
ing balancing and commissioning to build a better understand-
ing of engineering this portion of the system.
Achieving accuracy requires full hydraulic analysis of all
system components during the design stage and inclusion of
contingency methods to ensure that accuracy.

April 2002|ASHRAE Journal
IEQ and the Impact
On Building Occupants
About the Authors
R
By Satish Kumar, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE, and William J. Fisk, P.E., Member ASHRAE
esearch into indoor environmental quality (IEQ) and its effects on
health, comfort and performance of occupants is becoming increas-
ingly essential. Facility managers are interested in IEQ’s close relation-
ship to energy use. Employers hope to enhance employee comfort and
productivity, reduce absenteeism and health-care costs, and reduce risk
of litigation. The rising interest in this field has placed additional pressure
on the research community for practical guidelines on creating a safe,
healthy and comfortable indoor environment.
Research on the relationships of IEQ
to the health, comfort, and productivity
of occupants has advanced considerably
within the last decade. A primary goal of
the Indoor Health and Productivity (IHP)
Project is to communicate the results of
this research (currently reported prima-
rily in research publications) to build-
ing professionals. Consequently, the IHP
project has worked with a peer review
panel to select five key IHP papers and
prepare summaries of these papers for
ASHRAE Journal. More information
about the objectives of the IHP project,
including an online bibliography, can be
found at www.IHPCentral.org.
This article precedes those summary
articles, which will appear in future is-
sues of ASHRAE Journal. This article
summarizes the methodology used to
select the papers, briefly summarizes the
message of each paper and discusses the
practical implications for architects and
engineers.
requested not to include their own pa-
pers in the short list. Finally, the steering
committee, seeking a broad portfolio,
selected the final five papers from the
seven papers receiving the highest num-
ber of recommendations.
Identifying Research Priorities
To gain insight into the current IEQ
literature rated highly by the peer re-
view committee, all 26 candidate pa-
pers submitted were classified based on
the indoor environment variables being
investigated (shown along the rows in
Table 1) and on the associated health/
productivity outcomes (shown along the
columns in Table 1). The last row and
the last column show the total number
of distinct papers appearing under each
row and column.
Ventilation rate/CO2 concentrations,
thermal conditions, and moisture or
dampness were the IEQ factors investi-
gated cited most often. Among the
health/productivity outcomes, sick
building syndrome symptoms were dis-
cussed in an overwhelming number of
studies (18 out of 26) followed by evalu-
ation of task performance, and occurrence
of allergy/asthma symptoms.
Satish Kumar, Ph.D., is a scientist at the En-
ergy Analysis Department at Lawrence Berkeley
National Laboratory, Washington, D.C.
William J. Fisk, P.E., is head of the Indoor En-
vironment Department at Lawrence Berkeley
National Laboratory, Berkeley, Calif.
Methodology
The IHP steering committee developed
criteria for selecting the papers, includ-
ing the following: relevance to IHP goals;
originality, novelty, quality of research
approach, and value of the paper to archi-
tects and engineers. The last of these cri-
teria was considered most important.
With input from the IHP technical staff,
the steering committee also selected an
international panel of 14 peer reviewers,
who are respected scientists and engi-
neers with knowledge of the IHP field.
The IHP technical staff nominated sev-
eral papers and each peer reviewer nomi-
nated two to three papers, resulting in a
pool of 26 candidate papers. All the can-
didate papers are listed in References.
Each candidate paper plus written jus-
tifications for the nominations were dis-
tributed to all peer reviewers. Each peer
reviewer then selected five papers from
the 26 candidate papers. To avoid any
conflict of interest, peer reviewers were
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, April, 2002.
ASHRAE Journal|April 2002
51
IEQ
lighting with productivity. Since the design, construction, and
operation of buildings is often driven by the desire to minimize
costs, the economic estimates in this paper should be of interest
to architects, engineers, facility managers, and employers.
The paper by Heschong (1999) relates a physical environ-
mental parameter—daylighting in school classrooms—to stan-
dardized test scores of students at a time when test scores are
driving school budget decisions. The finding of this study was
a ~20% larger increase in test scores in classes with more
daylighting. If replicated in future studies, this would provide
a compelling case for increased daylight in classrooms.
Minimum ventilation requirements are of much interest to
building engineers and operators and have been a controver-
sial topic within ASHRAE. To date, these minimum ventila-
tion standards have had a limited scientific underpinning.
The papers by Seppanen et al. (1999) and Milton et al. (2000)
help to consolidate and solidify the scientific basis for the
development and refinement of ventilation standards. They
are particularly useful considering current energy shortages
and the renewed interest in reducing ventilation rates in build-
ings to save energy.
These papers indicate that higher ventilation rates will, on
average, improve occupants’ health, reduce absences, and im-
prove perceived air quality. The papers provide considerable
evidence of benefits from increasing office ventilation rates
Only two papers from the
pool estimated economic
gains from improvements in
health and productivity. In-
clusion of both emphasizes
the importance of this topic
and the need for more re-
search on these outcomes.
Although eight studies
discussed enhancement of
task performance from im-
proved indoor environ-
ments, they focused on ei-
ther component skills that
represent a small subset of
the range of activities per-
formed by people in work
environments or on perfor-
mance metrics such as stan-
dardized test scores of
school students that cannot
be used in many indoor en-
vironments. Few studies
have investigated the effects
of indoor environmental
parameters on the overall
productivity of non-indus-
trial workers. The gaps in
Table 1, where the peer review committee identified no or very
few top-rated papers, highlight areas of research need.
Commentary on the Five Final Papers
Of the five final papers, one estimated potential health ben-
efits and economic gains from practical improvements in IEQ
(Fisk 2000), one investigated the relationships of daylighting
with students’ performance (Heschong 1999), two (Milton et
al. 2000; Seppanen et al. 1999) addressed relationships of ven-
tilation rate to the health of building occupants, and the last
paper reported associations between characteristics of HVAC
systems and self-reported health symptoms (Sieber et al. 1996).
All of these papers were published in the last five years.
Fisk (2000) summarizes available research on the major in-
door environment factors affecting human health and produc-
tivity. For the U.S., this paper estimates that health effects ex-
perienced by millions of people annually could be signifi-
cantly reduced by improving IEQ, with associated annual eco-
nomic benefits of tens of billions of dollars. The paper indi-
cates that improvements in lighting and thermal conditions
may lead to additional, even larger, productivity gains.
The paper reviews the literature on the relationships of IEQ
with communicable respiratory disease, allergies and asthma,
and sick building syndrome symptoms. It also briefly reviews
the literature on the relationships of thermal conditions and

cThese studies were conducted in home environment and were included as candidate papers because reviewers
believed that the findings of the papers may have some relevance to non-industrial work environment as well.
\*The numbers within the table refer to specific papers in the reference section. The numbers of the five selected
papers are shown in bold type.

April 2002|ASHRAE Journal
above those specified in ANSI/ASHRAE Standard 62-1999,
Ventilation for Acceptable Indoor Air Quality.
HVAC maintenance deficiencies and HVAC contamination
have been suspected risk factors for health symptoms, but re-
lated scientific research has been limited. The paper by Sieber
et al. (1996), based on a study of complaint buildings, is one of
the few indicating the importance of HVAC cleanliness and
maintenance for human health.
The paper also reported that pollutant sources located near
outside air intakes increased the risk of adverse health effects.
The analyses controlled for the effects of age and gender on
health symptoms but the study was not able to identify which
HVAC cleanliness or maintenance conditions actually caused
an increase in health effects.
Taken together, these five papers increase the strength of
available scientific evidence that IEQ substantially affects
health and productivity. Each of these studies had some limi-
tations that will be discussed in the summary articles to be
published in subsequent issues of ASHRAE Journal. While
more research is clearly needed, the message to architects
and engineers is to pay attention to IEQ, in particular to en-
suring minimum ventilation rates, because many studies have
found that ventilation rates influence health, satisfaction with
indoor air quality or absences.
Future Research
In the last 20 years, IEQ researchers have substantially ad-
vanced our understanding of links between enhanced health
and productivity and improved IEQ, but many uncertainties
remain about the costs and benefits of specific measures. Con-
sequently, a critical need exists for more research to quantify the
relationships of IEQ to health and productivity, define accept-
able IEQ, and the best methods and costs of improving IEQ. The
most effective research in this field will be multidisciplinary,
involving building engineers, physical scientists, health scien-
tists, economists, etc. In addition, research is needed on how to
best stimulate building professionals to use available scientific
knowledge to create healthy building environments.

About the Author
dding outdoor air in hot, humid climates causes moisture prob-
lems, right? The answer is sometimes. It all depends on the condi-
tion of the house before outdoor air is added. In fact, most houses in
hot, humid climates are over-ventilated due to duct leakage and in-
duced air change from internal air pressure effects.
Outdoor air is added to a building via
a controlled ventilation system. What
isn’t controlled is the air change created
by wind effects, stack effects and pres-
sure effects caused by the operation of
the HVAC system.
In most houses, these uncontrolled ef-
fects typically are larger than the desired
controlled ventilation. Adding outdoor
air only makes a bad situation worse.
The desired controlled ventilation is
quantified here as:
7.5 cfm/person + 0.01 cfm/ft2 of
conditioned floor area
Occupancy is defined as the number
of bedrooms + 1
This value comes from proposed
ASHRAE Standard 62.2P, Ventilation for
Acceptable Indoor Air Quality in Low-
Rise Residential Buildings. The merits
of this controlled ventilation rate are not
discussed here. For a common single-
family detached house (three bedrooms,
2,000 ft2 [186 m2]), this works out to 50
cfm (24 L/s) of outdoor air. Restated,
based on typical house volumes (ceiling
heights ranging between 8 ft and 10 ft
words, the building envelopes were
fairly “leaky” for new homes, but fairly
“tight” for older homes. Remember, air
change does not occur without an air
pressure difference — even in a “leaky”
building enclosure. In the humid South,
stack effects and average wind speeds
acting on low-rise residential enclosures
are small, which means air change due
to these effects is low, and almost inde-
pendent of envelope leakage area for
new homes with this range of leakage
characteristics.
During the past decade, residential
building envelope leakage areas have
decreased due to more effective draft
stopping. This has resulted in an unin-
tended consequence. Air pressure differ-
ences within houses and between houses
and the exterior have increased when
HVAC systems are operating. Although
the airtightness of the building envelope
has increased, the expected air change
reduction has not occurred due to the
induced air pressure effects. Houses with
leaky ductwork and unbalanced airflows
continue to have high air change rates
despite improvements in building enve-
lope construction.
Pressure Effects
HVAC system-induced air change oc-
curs two ways: leaky ductwork and air
handlers located outside of the build-
By Joseph Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Member ASHRAE
A
[2.4 m and 3 m]), this is between 0.15
and 0.2 air changes per hour.
Wind effects and stack effects acting
on residential enclosures in hot, humid
climates are low.1 Most houses in hot,
humid climates are one story. Therefore,
only a small stack potential exists even
if an appreciable temperature difference
is present. The houses are often clad with
stucco, which is a tight cladding system
that is relatively wind insensitive.
Research done in the late 1980s and
early 1990s2,3 showed that, in homes
located in hot, humid climates, HVAC
system operation yielded air change
rates of between 0.5 and 1.0. When
HVAC systems were not operating the
same research showed air change rates
of 0.1 to 0.2 ACH.
Duty cycles (on time) often range be-
tween 30% and 70%. Assuming a 33%
duty cycle, the HVAC system induced air
change is equal to a continuous ventila-
tion rate of between 50 cfm and 100 cfm
(24 and 47 L/s).
At that time, the typical house enve-
lope leakage characteristics for new
homes ranged between 2,000 and 3,000
cfm (944 and 1416 L/s) at 50 Pa. In other

with Building Science Corporation, Westford, Mass.

ing pressure boundary (Figure 1a) and unbalanced airflows
due to door closure and inadequate return flow (Figure 1b). A
subset of the first type of air change also occurs due to air
pressure effects within building cavities (Figure 1c). This
type of induced infiltration results in serious interstitial mois-
ture problems, but does not always constitute a significant
interior latent load.
Addressing uncontrolled HVAC system-induced air change
requires tight ductwork, sealed air handlers, and balancing the
flow between rooms. When these requirements are met, uncon-
trolled air change can be reduced to nearly zero.
Ductwork and air handler leakage can be addressed using
many techniques that will not be addressed here (except to
say that standard duct tape is not one of them). Leakage val-
ues of approximately 5% (as tested by pressurization at 25
Pa) of total flow or less appear to result in negligible air
change when HVAC systems are operating and return system
flows are balanced.6 Many federal, state and utility construc-
tion programs have promoted duct system and air handler
leakage performance specifications in the 5% range. This
range of duct leakage appears to be justified based on the
field experience.
Pressurization effects due to door closure can cause sig-
nificant air change and comfort problems. Bedroom pressur-
ization relative to hallway/common areas of 10 to 20 Pa is
common with single return systems.5,7 These effects can
be mitigated using transfer grilles (Figure 2a) or jump ducts
(Figure 2b).
The New Moisture Balance
If uncontrolled air change is reduced to nearly zero and
replaced with a continuous flow roughly equal to the uncon-
trolled air change, it can be argued that the net moisture bal-
ance should remain unaffected. Adding controlled ventilation
does not increase latent loads under this condition.
However, this is only true during the months when HVAC
systems regularly operate. During spring and fall, the “part load”
seasons, HVAC systems do not operate regularly. Air change
drops to between 0.1 and 0.2 ACH. The typical house has a very
low latent load due to air change during this time. This is fortu-
nate because there is also almost no provision for moisture re-
moval during this time by the HVAC system. Adding controlled
ventilation significantly increases the latent load. In fact, the
load during this time is almost completely latent because the

April 2002|ASHRAE Journal
outdoor dry-bulb temperature is very near to the indoor dry-
bulb temperature. This latent load is effectively doubled in the
typical house by adding a ventilation rate of 0.15 and 0.2 ACH
(the proposed ASHRAE Standard 62.2P rate).
Gatley4 notes that the process of air conditioning consists
of approximately 70% sensible cooling in the form of tem-
perature reduction accompanied by approximately 30% la-
tent cooling in the form of water vapor removal. According to
Gatley, at partial load operating conditions (which occur for
a majority of system operating hours) many dwellings in hot,
humid climates require higher ratios of latent cooling of 40%
to 50%. This is not possible with standard residential air-
conditioning equipment.
The latent load problem under part-load conditions during
spring and fall in humid climates progressively is becoming
more serious as the typical residential building envelope and
its mechanical equipment evolve. The use of high-performance
window systems (spectrally selective windows with high vis-
ible light transmittance, low solar heat gain coefficient) is sig-
nificantly increasing the time part-load conditions occur during
the year. As a final insult to the house and its occupants, the
equipment is typically oversized (on the order of 150% or
more of total load). Adding controlled ventilation with un-
treated outdoor air to a house under these conditions almost
ensures problems — even with tight ductwork, sealed air han-
dlers and balanced airflows.
Gatley, citing work done by an air-conditioning system
manufacturer,4 shows the effect of oversizing. Systems over-
sized by 20% achieve latent heat removal of approximately
15% of the total cooling load under the assumed part-load
conditions. Whereas, correctly sized systems achieve mois-
ture removal of up to approximately 30% under part-load
conditions. The same work shows that correctly sized sys-
tems with variable speed fans and constant speed refrigera-
tion compressors achieve 35% moisture removal under
part-load conditions. With variable refrigeration compres-
sion, moisture removal is even greater.
Equipment sizing is not addressed here except to note that
it is a major residential HVAC industry problem. Even with
correctly sized standard HVAC systems, the part-load prob-
lem associated with the latent load remains, albeit not to the
same extent.
The author’s experience with newly constructed houses in
hot, humid climates has led to the conclusion that supplemen-
tal dehumidification during part-load periods is necessary —
especially in houses with controlled ventilation.
Three general approaches to supplemental dehumidification
in residences are recommended: stand-alone dehumidifiers (Fig-
ure 3); enhanced air-conditioning systems (variable speed blow-
ers and compressors); and ventilating dehumidifiers.
The use of a stand-alone dehumidifier plumbed/drained to a
condensate drain has the advantage of low cost (adds approxi-
! Air handler located and accessed within
interior conditioned space.
" Dehumidifier and condensate overflow
pan supported by metal stand. Dehumidifier
drain plumbed to air handler condensate
system.
# Return ducted with two offsets to reduce

$ Outside air provided to return side of
system with motorized damper control.
Flow controller for outside air should not
allow air handler to run with motorized damper
open for 15 minutes after cooling is de-ener-
gized to prevent re-evaporation of conden-
sate from coils and drain pan.
Outside air is controlled by a motorized
damper. Closing the outside air damper dur-
ing unoccupied periods will allow the flow con-
troller to periodically mix the interior air with-
out bringing in outside air helping the dehu-
midifier control interior RH — humid air is
brought to the dehumidifier. The cooling func-
tion of the air conditioner also can be shut
down during this time (i.e., the unit on blower-
only operation).

by duct leakage.

Ventilation
mately $300 to the typical house). The disadvantage is that
heat generated by the dehumidifier can affect comfort. Addi-
tionally, operating costs are the highest of the three general
approaches. However, it is a simple to implement solution and
typically compensates for oversizing of equipment.
The advantage of enhanced air-conditioning systems is
that a separate piece of equipment does not have to be in-
stalled. The disadvantage is a substantial increase in cost
(adds approximately $1,000 or more to the typical house).
Additionally, equipment sizing is critical. However, imple-
mentation is the easiest of the three systems and costs likely
will drop, as these systems become commodity items as op-
posed to special orders.
The use of ventilating dehumidifiers may have the advan-
tage of the lowest operating cost (since these dehumidifiers
have been optimized for humidity control, and some
also provide limited cooling capacity). The chief disadvan-
tage is their current cost (adds approximately $2,000 to the
typical house).
Conclusions
To provide controlled ventilation in new houses in hot, hu-
mid climates without creating latent load problems, it is nec-
essary to take the following measures:
• Install tight ductwork and air handlers,
• Balance interior airflows,
• Size equipment correctly, and
• Provide supplemental dehumidification.
