C
By William D. Gerstler, Ph.D., Member ASHRAE
ommercial kitchen design professionals were concerned
that the minimum exhaust air ductwork velocity of 1,500
fpm (7.62 m/s), as required by the National Fire Protection
Association (NFPA 961), was too restrictive. ASHRAE Tech-
nical Committee 5.10, Kitchen Ventilation, sponsored and
provided project guidance for a recently completed research
project, RP-1033, addressing the relationship between grease deposi-
tion and exhaust velocity. The research project not only resulted in
several published documents,2,3,4 but NFPA 96 reduced the minimum
exhaust velocity to 500 fpm (2.54 m/s) in March 2002.
important parameters, resulting in an
improved understanding of the deposi-
tion processes. From this information em-
pirical models of the processes are
developed. Actual cooking effluent mea-
surements help validate the models for a
limited “real-world” case (see sidebar on
Page 30 for more information about the
setup and procedure).
Results
Deposition velocity is the fundamen-
tal parameter used to describe the rate at
which particles deposit to a surface. It is
analogous to a mass transfer coefficient
and is calculated using Equation 1.

This article summarizes the study that
influenced the change in NFPA 96 and
presents examples of how to benefit from
the less restrictive requirements while
avoiding potential pitfalls.
Cooking effluent consists of vapors
and particles. Previous experience5,6 in-
dicates that using real cooking processes
to produce cooking effluent for measure-
ment purposes is expensive, uncontrol-
lable, and leads to highly variable
emission rates. For this reason, the ex-
perimental approach to address grease
deposition in exhaust ductwork is to
measure particulate and vapor deposi-
tion rates separately using artificial cook-
ing effluent. This allows control over the

the particle mass concentration. For this
investigation, the mass concentration
was calculated using the measured aero-
sol concentration at the duct centerline.

using Equation 2.

Where m is the particle mass deposited
on the surface, A is the surface area, and t
is the accumulated collection time. The
deposition velocity is of practical use in
comparing particle deposition in real-
world applications where the aerosol con-
centration remains constant. For example,
when a high emission kitchen appliance
is replaced with a low emission appliance,
and the total exhaust volume is reduced,
the aerosol concentration can remain con-
stant. Likewise, when designing a new
exhaust system, it is possible to adjust the

New Rules
For Kitchen
Exhaust
An ASHRAE research project led to a new NFPA
exhaust velocity standard.

November 2002
ASHRAE Journal
2 7
duct size and corresponding volumetric flow rate while main-
taining constant aerosol concentration.
Some applications have a constant aerosol generation rate that
results in an increase in particle concentration when reducing the
exhaust velocity. For instance, an appliance may be found to
operate effectively with reduced exhaust volumetric flow rate
while maintaining capture and containment. If the effluent emis-
sions and exhaust duct size remains the same, the result will not
only be a decrease in exhaust velocity, but a corresponding in-
crease in particle concentration. Therefore, the deposition veloc-
ity is not the only parameter of interest; the rise in concentration
in the exhaust also affects the particle deposition rate. Multiply-
ing the deposition velocity by the concentration before and after
a change in exhaust flow rate provides the mass flux to the wall in
each case, which allows for quantitative comparison. For discus-
sion purposes, the situation with constant aerosol concentration
is referred to as Case I, and the situation with a constant aerosol
generation rate is referred to as Case II.
In Figure 1, the ordinate is the deposition velocity while the
abscissa shows the collection area of the duct. Measurements
were taken on all four sides of the horizontal exhaust duct,
thus the areas are designated top, bottom, and left and right
sides. The overall average deposition also is shown. Each chart
shows results from all four tested exhaust velocities. Isother-
mal results for two particle sizes, 4.3 and 1.6 µm are shown
along with non-isothermal results for the 1.6 µm particles at
120°F and 150°F (48.9°C and 65.6°C) exhaust temperatures.
The data are directly applicable to Case I.
For isothermal cases, it is clear deposition velocity decreases
as the exhaust velocity decreases. This is true for both the
horizontal surfaces (top and bottom) and vertical surfaces

William D. Gerstler, Ph.D., is a mechanical engineer for General Electric’s
Global Research Center, Niskayuna, N.Y. The research was performed
while he was a graduate student at the University of Minnesota’s Mechanical
Engineering Department.

ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
November 2002
(left and right sides). The deposition is higher at the bottom
surface and lower at the top surface due to the effects of gravi-
tational settling. The sides are unaffected by gravitational
settling. The other deposition mechanism at work is called
turbulent aerosol deposition. Higher turbulence results in
more particles “thrown” to the wall surface. Lowering the
exhaust velocity does not affect gravitational settling, but
does decrease turbulent deposition dramatically. The net re-
sult is a decrease in deposition. While not shown in the fig-
ure, similar results were found for the two smaller particle
sizes, 0.95 and 0.49 µm.
Applying the result to Case II is more complicated. For
vertical surfaces, the deposition velocity decreases much more
rapidly than the particle concentration increases, thus there
is still a significant decrease in deposition with decreasing
exhaust velocity for all particle sizes tested. For the bottom
surface, higher gravitational settling due to higher particle
concentrations either cancels out the decrease in deposition
velocity (largest particles), or begins to dominate (smaller
particles). The net result for all internal surfaces of a horizon-
tal duct is a reduction in deposition with decreasing exhaust
velocity for the 4.3 µm particles, a constant deposition for
the 0.95 and 0.49 µm particles, and a minimal value of depo-
sition at about 1,000 fpm (5.08 m/s) with a slight increase as
the velocity goes to 500 fpm (2.54 m/s) for the 1.6 µm par-
ticles. The magnitude of the mass deposition is much greater
for the larger particles. When a wide size range of particles is
present, the behavior of the largest ones will dominate the
overall deposition. This suggests that in most cases, reducing
the exhaust volume will either decrease aerosol deposition
or not change it appreciably.
When the exhaust air temperature is elevated and there is
an appreciable wall-exhaust air temperature difference, the
phenomena of thermophoresis becomes an important deposi-
tion mechanism. Thermophoresis causes particles to migrate
from hot to cold air temperatures due to uneven thermally
induced collisions with gas molecules. The momentum from
the hot side air molecules is greater than the momentum from
the cold side air molecules and the particle moves towards the
cold. The charts for the elevated exhaust air temperature (C
and D) show that eventually thermophoresis begins to domi-
nate particle deposition for Case I applications. However, even
at the highest temperature difference tested, the deposition
velocity does not increase for horizontal surfaces, but remains
rather constant. When applied to Case II applications, deposi-
tion begins to increase with decreasing exhaust velocity, at
the wall-exhaust air temperature differences tested. Therefore,
the combination of increased particle concentration and
thermophoretic effects increasing deposition overwhelms the
effect of turbulent deposition effects decreasing deposition,
with decreasing exhaust velocity.
It is important to comment on the tested wall-exhaust tem-
perature differences and how likely they are to be experi-
enced in the field. The temperature difference for all 10 cook-
ing processes tested in a previous investigation5 exceeded
2.1°F (1.1°C) for only the gas broiler and gas range. In this
case, the exhaust duct was not insulated. Therefore, it is likely
that many commercial cooking exhaust systems do not ex-
perience significant temperature differences even with non-
insulated ducts.
Unlike particle deposition in turbulent airflow, turbulent
vapor deposition is a well-understood and documented phe-
nomena. Therefore, the results will not be presented here. It is
sufficient to report the results confirmed the applicability of
classic mass transfer theory. The theory predicts the transfer
rate decreases with decreasing exhaust velocity and decreas-
ing temperature difference between the exhaust air and the
duct. In non-insulated ducts, the temperature difference will
increase, increasing deposition, while the decreasing exhaust
velocity decreases deposition. The net result will be an in-
crease in deposition because the dependence on temperature
difference is more pronounced. For insulated ducts, the vapor
deposition will be negligible.
Summary
The following general observations can be made:
Case I: Constant Exhaust Concentration and Temperature
The rate of grease accumulation decreases with reduced ex-
haust velocity regardless of the duct orientation and insula-
tion level.
Case II: Constant Effluent Generation Rate, Variable Con-
centration in Exhaust
a) Well insulated ducts, R-10 h ft2 °F/Btu (1.8 m2 °C/W)
and higher,
The rate of grease accumulation decreases with reduced ve-
locity regardless of duct orientation.
b) Uninsulated ducts,
Here the results depend on the specific conditions. For high
velocities, the rate of total grease deposition will decrease
with a reduction in velocity when the particle deposition domi-
nates, but will increase for all other conditions.
Discussion and Application of Results
Three scenarios are presented using the results generated in
this investigation. The idea is to guide the designer and user of
kitchen exhaust duct systems regarding the influence of ex-
haust velocity on the rate of grease accumulation in the straight
sections of an exhaust duct.
A. Consider a situation in which a current exhaust system
runs continuously at 1,500 fpm (7.6 m/s) regardless of the
amount of effluent generated. Assume that the effluent genera-
tion consists of periods of full load and periods where a mini-
mal amount is generated. What is the impact of reducing the
exhaust velocity during the periods of very light load?
The majority of the grease deposition will occur during full-
load operation. A negligible amount will occur during the
Exhaust
November 2002
ASHRAE Journal
2 9
Measurements are made in a
10 by 10-in. (254 by 254-mm)
square exhaust duct made of
welded black iron. Exhaust ve-
locities are controlled by a vari-
able speed fan and include 500,
1,000, 1,500, and 2,000 fpm
(2.54, 5.08, 7.62, and 10.16
m/s). Two duct heaters allow the
control of test exhaust tempera-
tures at ambient, 120°F
(48.9°C), and 150°F (65.6°C).
Particulates are introduced 15-
ft (4.57 m) upstream of the test
measurement section using an
aerosol generator. The sche-
matic of the test setup is shown
in Figure 2.
For particulate generation, oleic acid is tagged with
uranine, a florescent dye, and mixed with various amounts
of isopropyl alcohol and DI water. The solution is sprayed
through a spray jet atomizer, resulting in polydispersed
test aerosols with mass median aerodynamic diameters
(MMAD) of 0.49, 0.95, 1.6, and 4.3 mm, depending on the
amount of volatiles in the solution. Oleic acid is appropri-
ate because it is often found in real cooking effluent. For
example, it was found to be the most prevalent fatty acid
in beef patties,5 and olive oil is 85% oleic acid.7 Uranine
enhances the deposition measurement by at least three
orders of magnitude over direct weighing. The technique
is widely used and well documented in aerosol deposi-
tion studies.8 The range of particle sizes associated with
each MMAD is characterized by the geometric standard
deviation (σg). The spray jet atomizer produces σg values
from 1.8 to 2.3, the same values found in cooking emis-
sion characterization studies. 5,6
The deposition of the particles is measured using 0.004
in. (0.102 mm) stainless steel shim stock attached to the
inner duct walls. After testing, the shim stock is carefully
removed, wiped down with isopropyl alcohol pads, and
the pads placed in a 0.0011 N solution of NaOH and DI
H2O. The solution is sonicated, allowed to settle, and the
mass of uranine determined fluorometrically. It should be
noted the airflow velocity, temperature, and aerosol con-
centration is fully characterized for each test. These pro-
cedures are briefly explained in a paper4 and explained in
detail in the research project full report.2
Using well-documented techniques in the heat transfer
literature, the grease vapor mass transport rate is deter-
mined by measuring the evaporation rate of a volatile
fatty acid from the duct wall into the exhaust airflow. It is
easier to establish well-characterized boundary conditions,
in which the vapor is transported from the duct wall into
the airflow, than the usual case in kitchen exhaust ducts
where the vapor flows towards the wall surface. The mag-
nitude of the mass transport coefficient is not affected by
this change in transport direction. Octanoic acid was cho-
sen as the fatty acid because of its high vapor pressure and
presence in many vegetable oils and butter. It is applied to
substrates located on the bottom of the duct test section. A
calibrated electric heater maintains the oil at a constant
temperature. The change of weight of the substrate during
each test is used to determine mass transport. The proce-
dure includes an experimentally determined correction
for evaporation due to initial weighting, mounting time,
preheat and post cooling, and final weighing procedures.
For the actual cooking effluent test, a small commercial
cooking electric broiler is used to cook ground beef pat-
ties. The food product specifications and cooking proce-
dures are specified in ASTM F-1695-96.9 The same
procedures used to characterize the effluent concentra-
tion, airflow, and deposition for the particulate study are
used for the actual cooking effluent test. However, the
cooking effluent is not tagged with a fluorescent dye;
therefore, deposition is measured gravimetrically. Please
refer to the full report for complete documentation of the
test setup and procedures.
Setup and Procedure

ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
November 2002
periods of light load regardless of the exhaust velocity. Thus,
the value of the exhaust velocity during periods of light load
has a negligible effect on the total grease buildup and could
be varied to capture and contain the effluent without regard to
the rate of grease buildup in the duct. The operating cost will
be dramatically reduced if there are significant downtimes.
Savings not only come from less fan energy, but a reduced
load for the makeup air system.
B. Consider a situation where the effluent is generated dur-
ing three periods; full load, two-thirds full load and one-third
full load. Assume that the effluent is identical, only the quan-
tity changes (e.g. using three, two, or one broiler respectively
to cook beef patties). The exhaust velocity is currently set at a
constant value of 1,500 fpm (7.6 m/s). Consider the effect of
grease accumulation in the existing duct if the velocity is
reduced to 1,000 fpm (5.1 m/s) during the two-thirds full-load
operation and to 500 fpm (2.5 m/s) during the one-third full-
load operation. Assume that the reduced velocity is adequate
for capture and containment so the only concern is the grease
accumulation rate in the exhaust duct.
This is an example of a Case I situation where the exhaust
concentration and temperature differences remain constant,
but the mean velocity changes. The rate of grease deposition
will decrease compared with the case of constant velocity for
all conditions. The rate of particle deposition decreases and
the rate of vapor deposition decreases for all duct configura-
tions, horizontal and vertical, insulated or not. Again, operat-
ing cost savings could be significant.
C. Consider a situation in which the effluent generation is
constant and the current operation is to maintain the exhaust
velocity at 1,500 fpm (7.6 m/s). How will the grease deposition
rate change if the velocity through the existing duct system is
reduced to 1,000 fpm (5.1 m/s)? Assume that capture and con-
tainment can be achieved with the reduced velocity. There-
fore, the grease deposition rate in the duct is the only concern.
Here the effluent generation rate is constant as the velocity
is varied so this corresponds to an example of Case II. The
concentration of particles and grease vapor increase as the
exhaust velocity decreases. The results depend on the amount
of duct insulation.
i. Well insulated duct, R-10 h ft2 °F/Btu (1.76 m2 °C/W) or
higher.
The vapor deposition rate is negligible, and the particle
deposition rate decreases with the reduced velocity so the rate
of total grease deposition decreases with reduced velocity for
all duct orientations.
ii. Uninsulated duct,
The particle deposition rate decreases but the rate of vapor
deposition increases. The net effect depends on the ratio of
particle to vapor deposition. In most cases, the particle deposi-
tion rate is expected to dominate so the total deposition rate
will decrease or increase slightly as the velocity is reduced. If
the velocity is further reduced to 500 fpm (2.5 m/s), the par-
ticle deposition rate may increase compared to the 1,000 fpm
(5.1 m/s) velocity because thermophoresis begins to domi-
nate. Therefore, the total rate of grease accumulation is ex-
pected to rise. The reader is referred to the model and example
calculation presented in the full report2 to quantify the ex-
pected deposition.
Scenario C has the greatest potential for problems arising
from improper application. For instance, a restaurant in Min-
neapolis may operate an exhaust system that is determined to
capture and contain effluent sufficiently at 50% of the current
flowrate. However, the exhaust velocity is 1,500 fpm (7.62 m/
s) and until recently, a reduction was not allowed. With the
new standard it is possible to reduce the velocity to 750 fpm
(3.81 m/s). However, at this restaurant, the exhaust exits the
building immediately into a non-insulated exhaust duct run-
ning outside the building. Due to the cold climate, the tem-
perature difference between the exhaust air and wall would be
significant, and would increase with a decrease in exhaust
velocity. The result would be an increase in both vapor and
particle deposition. It is likely that the restaurant already has
grease buildup problems at 1,500 fpm (7.62 m/s), especially in
midwinter. Reducing the exhaust velocity would compound
this. The best solution would be to insulate the duct.
Other Considerations
The results presented are applicable to straight rectangular
or round ductwork. Only when a high aspect ratio horizontal
duct is used would we expect significantly different results
(due to an increased surface area for gravitational settling).
Of more significance is the effect of bends, elbows, or other
non-straight ductwork, which usually are present in exhaust
systems. The vapor deposition will remain unchanged flow-
ing through these regions. It is expected that the particle
deposition will decrease significantly with decreased exhaust
velocity. The explanation is particle deposition in a bend is
dominated by inertial impaction. Inertial impaction is a func-
tion of velocity and decreases with decreasing velocity. Dur-
ing the actual cooking tests, a substrate was placed on the
inner surface of the 90-degree bend, downstream of the test
section. The results show less deposition at lower velocities.
Therefore, while testing exhaust velocity effects in elbows
and bends was beyond the scope of the ASHRAE research
project, there is strong theoretical evidence, and some ex-
perimental evidence, that a reduction in velocity decreases
grease deposition.
Finally, all examples assume that effluent capture and con-
tainment is satisfied and the minimum velocity for the grease
removal device is met when the exhaust volume is reduced. In
practice this a critical element and airflow reduction should
only be considered if the exhaust hood properly captures and
contains at the new flow rate. If the velocity required for the
grease removal device cannot be met with the lowered exhaust
volume, one can reduce the effective area of the grease re-
Exhaust
November 2002
ASHRAE Journal
3 1
moval device, proportionally increasing the velocity. For in-
stance, with baffle filters, blanks are often available, and can
be placed in an area of the hood with minimal direct contact
with effluent.
Conclusions
An experimental investigation of exhaust velocity effects
on grease deposition in kitchen exhaust ducts has influenced
changes to the NFPA 96 minimum exhaust requirement. The
requirement has been reduced from 1,500 fpm (7.6 m/s) to 500
fpm (2.5 m/s). The new standard maintains or improves the
safety aspect of minimizing grease build-up, while allowing
engineers more flexibility in both retrofit and new kitchen
design, including variable flow kitchen exhaust. For many
applications, a reduction in exhaust velocity will be benefi-
cial. In some cases, lowering the exhaust velocity can increase
grease deposition and the design professional should be care-
ful to identify these situations. Lowering velocity reduces
grease deposition in virtually all cases when ductwork with
insulation of R-10 or greater is used.
References

and fire protection of commercial cooking operations. Quincy, Mass.:

3. Gerstler, W.D. 2000. “Turbulent aerosol deposition in large and

4. Gerstler, W.D., et al. 2002. “The effects of exhaust air velocity on
grease deposition in kitchen exhaust ductwork.” ASHRAE Transactions

A
ir often moves through buildings in ways
no one intended or expected. No one
plans for air to come down the chimney, spill-
ing the products of combustion into occu-
pied space. No one plans for ventilating air to
gather moisture and particles by flowing
through a crawlspace before entering the
building. No arrows on drawings indicate that
air should flow from the boiler room through
utility chases until it reaches the top floor
where it enters occupied space through gaps
around pipes and conduits. And yet airflows
like these occur in nearly every building.
Buildings are composed of chambers con-
Figure 1: Buildings are a distributed resistance/pressure-filled system driven by fans,
buoyancy of heated air and wind.10
nected by air leaks. Rooms are chambers. Hall-
ways are chambers. Stud walls are chambers.
Plenums, soffits, chases and shafts all provide
passages for airflows. The room you occupy is
one element in a complex, distributed resis-
tance/pressure difference network. Air moves
from one chamber to another because of air
pressure differences. The amount of air that
moves depends on the pressure difference and
the size of the holes. Many connecting holes
are hidden. The holes in studs, the gap between
the gypsum board and the sill plate, electric
outlets, and open tops of hollow core concrete
masonry units are not obvious airflow paths.
By Terry Brennan, James B. Cummings, Member ASHRAE,
and Joseph Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., Member ASHRAE
Problem
Areas
Riser
Riser connects to a return ple-
num to an AHU on the roof.
It opens to many leakage sites
and is filled with plumbing.
AHU Soffit
The fiberglass insulation in
the lower portion of the photo
is stained with particles from
the air. Negative pressure in
the kitchen below draws air
from the attic, through the
fiberglass, into the soffit, into
the cavity and out around
electrical outlets. The par-
ticles reveal the direction of
the airflow.
Dust on Door
Dust deposit on door jamb
indicates that when the door
is closed air passes from the
room at the right of the photo
around the door and into the
room at left. Dust is depos-
ited by inertial forces after the
air makes the corner between
the stop and the jamb.

45
Moisture
Doors and windows are not only obvious
paths—they are bimodal. Their resistances
may be rather large or small depending on
whether they are open or closed. The leakage
area of an open door is a few thousand square
inches that can change to less than a hundred
square inches when closed. The airflow pat-
terns in a building may change completely
depending on whether a door is open or closed.
Buildings connected to each other by tunnels
or passageways become linked networks. The
network extends through openings in foun-
dations into the underlying soil, sometimes
resulting in air entering the building from be-
low grade.
Most airflows are powered by fans, by dif-
ferences in air density or by the wind. The
motion of elevator cars in their shafts may
draw air in behind them and push it out ahead
of them. A change in the water level in an
underground cavern presses or pulls large
amounts of air through the soil, cavern sys-
tem and shattered bedrock. The combination
of heavy rainfall and a low-pressure system
moving often causes a spike of soil air to pass
through buildings. Figure 1 illustrates the dis-
tributed resistance network model of airflow
in buildings.
Airflow quantities and directions depend
on the operational state of air-handling equip-
ment, the position of dampers, the location of
supply diffusers and return/exhaust grilles, the
position of doors and windows, wind direc-
tion and speed and the temperature difference
between the indoor and outdoor air.
Often unplanned airflows have no adverse
impact on occupants or the building. Some-
times, they can have a positive impact. For
example, air leaks between the return plenum
and the outdoors may provide ventilating air
to rooms that would otherwise have no venti-
lation. One of the authors once saw an 80,000
ft2 (7400 m2) school that did not have elevated
radon in every room because the boiler room
depressurized the perimeter utility trenches,
accidentally preventing soil air from enter-
ing the classrooms in the same way that ra-
don control systems do intentionally.
However, unplanned airflows induced in the
building envelope cavities are at the heart of
many problems with air quality, thermal com-
fort, excessive energy use and moisture. These
are the airflows that are important to us. They
may be important because a large volume of
air is involved or they may be important be-
cause a small flow results in a bad conse-
quence.
For example, if outdoor air is accidentally
drawn into a return plenum when it is hot
and humid outdoors, an accidental latent
and sensible load is introduced. Then, if that
return plenum draws hot, humid outdoor air
through wall cavities whose gypsum board
skins are chilled by air conditioning, it’s
likely that condensation will wet the back-
side of the gypsum board. If the paper stays
wet long enough, mold will grow. Fungal
spores and their gaseous metabolites will be

James B. Cummings is a program director at the Florida Solar Energy Center in Cocoa, Fla. He is chair of
ASHRAE Journal/Insights Committee.
Joseph Lstiburek, Ph.D., P.Eng., is a principal with Building Science Corporation, Westford, Mass.
Doors and windows are not only obvious paths—they are
bimodal. Their resistances may be rather large or small
depending on whether they are open or closed. The leak-
age area of an open door is a few thousand square inches
that can change to less than a hundred square inches when
closed. The airflow patterns in a building may change com-
pletely depending on whether a door is open or closed.
‘
’
Pipe Penetration
Gaps around pipes, ducts and
conduits are common.
Isolated Elevator
The atrium and elevators are
separated from the corridors
and rooms in this building.
To reduce the impact stack
effect has on tall multistory
buildings
during
cold
weather, the building must
be compartmentalized. Each
floor of rooms must be iso-
lated from the shafts in the
building—stairwells, atria,
elevator shafts and utility
chases.
And More
Problem Areas...
Multistack Buildings
Stack effect in this three-story
building, in combination with
poor heat control, causes first-
floor occupants to keep their
windows closed and heat on
full. Occupants on the top
floor have the heat off and the
windows open. The ventilat-
ing air is humidified as it
passes from floor to floor.
46
ASHRAE Journal
November 2002
released into the airstream that deposited the moisture, which
is headed towards the air handler, combining contaminant
source and transport mechanism into one convenient pack-
age. This article focuses on moisture-related problems that
result from unplanned airflows.
How Large are These Airflows?
A small number of studies have been able to distinguish
between unplanned and planned airflows. In the 1980s re-
searchers at the National Institute of Standards and Technol-
ogy,1 studied a three-story office building. The building had
two VAV air handlers to condition and ventilate. As part of the
study, the ventilating air passing through the outdoor air in-
takes on the air handlers was measured using pitot tube and
hot wire anemometers, while at the same time the total ventila-
tion rate was measured using tracer decay techniques.
The system had three modes of operation providing three
different amounts of outdoor air: outdoor air dampers at a
fraction of minimum, outdoor air dampers at minimum and
outdoor air dampers fully open. When the dampers were set
at the sub-minimum position, 58% (6,100 cfm [2878 L/s]), of
the ventilating air was due to infiltration through the enve-
lope. At minimum outdoor air damper position, 31% (3,600
cfm [1698 L/s]) was due to infiltration, and at maximum 49%
(18,000 cfm [8500 L/s]) was through infiltration rather than
by intended ventilation.
At about the same time, researchers at Lawrence Berkeley
Laboratory2,3 used a constant injection tracer method to esti-
mate the total ventilation rates and to measure the ventilation
through the air-handling equipment. They reported that un-
planned airflows make up from 0% to 30% of the ventilating
air in the buildings they studied.
Later, a systematic study of unplanned airflows in non-resi-
dential buildings was conducted by the Florida Solar Energy
Center.4 This study measured unplanned airflows in 70 non-
residential buildings. Many aspects of unplanned airflows were
investigated in this study. Among the results:
• Ductwork was around 70 times leakier than the SMACNA
standard (1.2 cfm at 25 Pa per 100 ft2 of duct area);
• Repairing unplanned airflows in 20 of the buildings re-
sulted in 15% energy savings;
• T-bar suspended ceilings are 10 times leakier than gypsum
board ceilings; and
• With the air handlers operating, eight of the buildings
have total ventilation rates between two and 10 air changes
per hour.
It’s clear from these studies that accidental airflows are often a
significant fraction of the total ventilation rate. Inflated ventila-
tion rates cause problems with comfort, energy use and mois-
ture. During heating conditions, inflated ventilation may reduce
indoor humidity levels below the comfort zone. In cooling or
dehumidification conditions, unplanned ventilation may add
excess moisture to the point of discomfort.
Moisture Dynamics and Unplanned Airflows
Condensation problems often are caused by accidental hu-
midification. Accidental humidification occurs when warm,
humid air flows across surfaces that are below the dew-point
temperature of the transported air. Water vapor in the air near
the chilled surface is cooled enough for condensation to oc-
cur, creating a partial pressure difference and often a convec-
tive loop. The chilled surface is accidentally dehumidifying
the air. Condensation is a problem if it wets moisture-sensitive
materials — those that grow mold, expand or deteriorate.
During the cooling season, the outdoors is the source of warm
humid air, while air-conditioning equipment and earth contact
chills the surfaces. When the temperatures of all the materials
inside buildings are lowered below the outdoor air dew point, it
is not surprising that accidental airflows result in condensation.
The colder the building, the more likely accidental airflows will
result in condensation. Earth chilled surfaces are more of a prob-
lem in cold climates with warm, humid summers.
If the source of the moisture is outdoor air, then accidental
depressurization (suction) is the driving force causing the prob-
lems. If the entire building is depressurized, outdoor air will be
drawn in through air leaks in the exterior envelope. The most
likely condensing surface is the backside of an interior vapor
retarder (e.g., vinyl wallpaper) or gypsum board. Less com-
monly, the inside of interior walls may generate condensation
if the building is on a crawl space that is humidified by poorly
managed rainwater, groundwater or outdoor air.
An entire building may become depressurized by several
mechanisms:
• The mechanical design is inappropriate for the climate
(e.g., exhaust-generated ventilation in a hot, humid climate).
• The mechanical system achieves positive pressurization
when filters are clean, but the building becomes negative when
clogged filters on outdoor air or makeup air intakes reduce
airflow into the building.
• During the day, the outdoor air dampers are open to pro-
vide ventilation. The dampers close at night, but toilet and
mechanical room exhaust continues to run, depressurizing the
building. Because the air conditioning is turned off at night,

(Interior Air
Pressure Field)
Bathroom
Fan Coil
Unit
Exterior Air
Pressure
Field
(Ambient or
Neutral
Pressure)
Dropped Ceiling Return Plenum
Partition Wall
(Demising Wall)
Exterior Wall
Corridor
Figure 2: An interior wall cavity depressurized by the return
plenum results in fungal growth.11
Moisture
November 2002
ASHRAE Journal
47
the building pulls in humid air without any dehumidification
all night long.
• The same dynamic occurs if the outdoor air dampers have
been disabled while the exhaust fans still run. This can happen
when outdoor air dampers are disabled in an effort to solve
other problems — excessive energy use or thermal comfort
problems.
• An otherwise well-designed
positive pressure system may be
turned into an exhaust-domi-
nated system when supply air
leaks outside the building’s
main air pressure boundary,
while at the same time there are
few return leaks. In effect, the
air handler is turned into an ex-
haust system.
• Sometimes, a building is
depressurized when connected
by another building by corri-
dors or tunnels. For example, an
older office building designed
when cooling was provided by
ventilation may have exhaust-
only ventilation. If it is con-
nected to a more modern
building that is air conditioned
with fan-powered outdoor air,
the modern building may be
completely depressurized by the
older one. Low-rise hotels de-
pressurized by exhaust fans in
swimming pool enclosures are
one such example. High-rise
hotels depressurized by unbal-
anced kitchen exhaust hoods
are another example.
Individual rooms may be-
come depressurized if supply
flow is out less than return or
exhaust flow. Central returns de-
pressurize portions of buildings
when doors are closed. If a room
is subdivided and the supply and return in the room also are
not subdivided, one room may go positive while the other
goes negative.
Last, wall and ceiling cavities may be depressurized by the
return side of the air-handling system. This is most common in
systems with return air plenums. In some cases this occurs
because the exterior wall cavities are part of a space intention-
ally used as a return plenum (e.g., mechanical room used as
return plenum). In other cases there is leakage from other build-
ing cavities into the plenum. There are often many holes in the
walls and overhead floors bounding the perimeter and top of a
return plenum. Gaps around pipes, ducts and conduit are com-
mon. Joints between corrugated roof deck and the tops of walls
offer large leaks. Even filled with mineral wool or fiberglass
there is still leakage. All it takes for a problem is for a signifi-
cant leakage path to the exterior. Figures 2 and 3 illustrate
return plenums depressurizing walls in two problem buildings
in hot, humid climates.
During heating conditions,
indoor moisture sources such
as people, pools, spas or hu-
midifiers provide warm, hu-
mid air. The cool surfaces are
found in the building enve-
lope, chilled by cold outdoor
air. There are three major dif-
ferences between cooling sea-
son and heating season
dynamics. First, the moisture
source and chilled surfaces
switch places and so the di-
rection of airflow changes.
Second, during cooling con-
ditions the driving force for
unplanned airflows is most fre-
quently air-handling equip-
ment, while during heating
conditions, it is just as likely
to be stack effect. Third, when
the air is cold and dry outside,
ventilating air dehumidifies
buildings, so any problems are
likely to be caused by excess
internal moisture. Museums,
some industrial processes,
poolrooms, spas, locker rooms
are typical of the uses more
likely to experience conden-
sation problems when air ac-
cidentally passes through the
envelope during cold weather.
Sometimes densely occupied
buildings like dormitories or
multi-family high-rises have these problems. Figure 4 illustrates
a condensation problem in a museum located in a cold climate.
Figure 5 illustrates a stack related humidity problem in a multi-
family building in a cold climate.
Preventing Unplanned Airflow Moisture Problems
There are three main rules for preventing moisture problems
caused by unplanned airflows:
• Keep warm, humid air away from chilled surfaces by mak-
ing certain all airflows are planned;
Moist air flows between
openings in masonry wall
and into air spaces be-
neath plaster.
Air pulled into dropped
ceiling due to negative
pressure from roof top ex-
haust and AHUs within
dropped ceiling.
Plaster
Wood furring strips
create air space.
Concrete Masonry
Wall
Baseboard
Precast Concrete
Floor Plank
Vented Crawl Space
Exposed Soil
Air from crawl space flows
between open joints in precast
concrete planks.
Soil moisture evaporates into
the crawl space.
Figure 3: Moist air from a crawlspace is drawn into wall
cavities by exhaust fans.12
Concrete Foundation Wall
Concrete
Footing
48
ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
November 2002
• Build wall and ceiling assemblies that are resistant to mois-
ture problems;
• Build mechanical systems that can remove the real dehu-
midification loads of the building.5
The FSEC study on unplanned airflows4 has several recom-
mendations for preventing them, including:
• Seal all ducts, plenums, connections to air handlers and
the building envelope itself
• Place air and thermal barriers in the same plane
• Do not place walls or other barriers between supply and
return diffusers and grilles
• Pressurize all rooms and cavities during hot, humid weather.
During cold weather (below 32°F [0°C]), it is better to slightly
depressurize if neutral pressure cannot be maintained);
• Include pressure mapping as part of test, adjust and bal-
ance (TAB) work;
• Establish operation and maintenance procedures that main-
tain the previous characteristics (including during renovations).
Proven methods and materials are available for sealing ducts,
plenums and envelopes. To ensure this occurs reliably, con-
struction documents should include tightness specifications
and methods of demonstrating tightness. SMACNA6 has a stan-
dard for duct tightness and a method for measuring duct leak-
age. ASTM7 has standard practices for measuring envelope
leakage. ASHRAE8 and EEBA9 have an envelope tightness
standard for residential envelope tightness.
In general, avoid plenum returns or at least operate return
plenums positive relative to outdoors, while negative relative
to room air. For example, during hot, humid weather, a build-
ing is best operated at 2 Pa with respect to outdoors while the
ceiling return plenum can operate at 1 Pa with respect to out-
doors. If it is not possible to avoid such return plenums, at least
make sure they are easy to seal up tightly with respect to air.
Sealing efforts conducted to avoid one problem may be inef-
fective for other purposes. Fire lagging may be adequate for
fire protection, but it can become permeable to air and mois-
ture flow and vulnerable to rodent attack. When return ple-
nums cannot be avoided, plan sealing efforts so that fire control,
airflow control, moisture migration and pest resistance are all
considered.
Placing air barriers and thermal barriers together either as
dual-purpose materials or as parallel layers prevents an “end
run” around the thermal barrier. For example, fiberglass insu-
lation laid on a suspended ceiling makes a thermal barrier, but
not an air barrier. Air can easily pass from the interior into
space above or from the space above into the conditioned space.
In cooling conditions, outdoor air may be accidentally drawn
into the building. In heating conditions, indoor air may enter
cold attic space causing condensation and in some climates,
ice dams.
Design the air-distribution system so rooms are always un-
der a slight positive pressure in hot, humid weather. However,
if the local climate has more than 8,000 heating degree days,
the rooms should be slightly depressurized.
During TAB, measure and report air pressure differences be-
tween rooms and corridors, walls, ceiling cavities, trenches,
crawl spaces, and chases. A single pressure map is insufficient.
Pressure maps must be made for each of the probable modes of
operation including combinations of mechanical system op-
erating cycles. For example, minimum and maximum outdoor
air damper position, exhaust fans on and off and thermostats
satisfied, calling for cooling and calling for heating. A test
procedure and the direction of airflow and pressure difference
must be specified. Subtracting supply flows from return flows
is not sufficient to determine pressure relationships, largely
because system flows through duct and plenum leaks must be
accounted for. Because of the difficulty involved, they are
seldom measured.
Finally, the authors believe that sooner or later, some por-
Figure 4: A condensation problem in a museum located in a
cold climate. Although many gallons of water condensed each
day, no fungal problems occurred. The only materials that
got wet were concrete masonry units, concrete floor, steel
studs and unfaced fiberglass insulation. None of these mate-
rials provide significant nutrients.

72°F, 41% RH
(°F – 32) ÷ 1.8 = °C; Pa ÷ 248.8 = in. of water (60°F)
Moisture
November 2002
ASHRAE Journal
49
tion of the inside of the walls in any building can become wet.
Architectural designers are encouraged to make the building
envelope tolerant of wetting and resistant to moisture prob-
lems. Specifically:
• Design walls and ceilings to dry easily to the inside, the
outside or both.
• Replace moisture sensitive materials with moisture insen-
sitive materials in locations that are more likely to get wet.
For example, in areas that are certain to get wet occasionally
such as bathrooms, basements or pool areas use materials that
are moisture insensitive. Paper covered gypsum board is very
sensitive to moisture. The new gypsum boards covered with
fiberglass fabric provide far less nutrient for fungal growth.
Foam insulating sheets are fairly resistant to moisture prob-
lems. Fiber-cement boards are resistant to mold growth.
Assemblies with poor drying potential such as sections with
low permeability materials on the inside and outside and or
those with cladding materials that store a lot of rainwater com-
bined with low perm, low R-value inner finish materials re-
main wet longer than those designed to dry. The oldest and
still most important rule is to avoid more than one vapor re-
tarder in an assembly. If water gets between them, there is no
place for it to go. The interaction of vapor retarders, air pres-
sure differences and vapor pressure differences must all be
considered if the wall is to dry quickly and easily after wetting
events. The drying characteristics of different assemblies can
be modeled or measured.
New materials and methods have turned what used to be
unimportant moisture dynamics into important moisture dy-
namics. By managing airflow direction, planning envelopes
that manage water vapor migration (being especially careful
using low R-value/low permeability materials and water ad-
sorptive claddings) and using sections that dry easily, these
problems can be avoided.

Legal Issues
66
ASHRAE Journal
November 2002
client has recently expanded into a nearby state, or a project has presented itself in another state. If you are a
professional engineer and you do not hold a current professional registration in the state in which the project is
located, what do you have to do to obtain licensure? Is it even required? This article addresses these questions and
the requirements for multi-state professional licensure by comity or endorsement.
By Duane A. Wolf, P.E., Associate Member ASHRAE
Licensing in Multiple Jurisdictions
gineering surveys, the coordination of services furnished by
structural, civil, mechanical and electrical engineers and other
consultants as they relate to engineering work and the inspec-
tion of construction for the purpose of compliance with draw-
ings and specifications, any of which embraces such service
or work either public or private, in connection with any utili-
ties, structures, buildings, machines, equipment, processes,
work systems, or projects and including such architectural work
as is incidental to the practice of engineering.”
In some states the definition of engineering services is not
First, is licensure required? If your anticipated activities will
fall within the definition of “practice as a professional engi-
neer,” the answer is “Yes.” A frequently used definition for the
practice of professional engineering is “providing any service
or creative work, the adequate performance of which requires
engineering education, training, and experience in the appli-
cation of special knowledge of the mathematical, physical,
and engineering sciences to such services or creative work as
consultation, investigation, evaluation, planning and design
of engineering works and systems, engineering teaching of
advanced engineering subjects or courses related thereto, en-
A
Duane Wolf is a Professional Engineer in Ames, Iowa. He is a member of
ASHRAE’s Task Group on General Legal Education.
The information contained in this article represents the opinion of
the author. It is not intended to, and does not, constitute legal advice,

Advertisement in the print edition formerly in this space.
The following article was published in ASHRAE Journal, November 2002. © Copyright 2002 American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-
Conditioning Engineers, Inc. It is presented for educational purposes only. This article may not be copied and/or distributed electronically or in paper
form without permission of ASHRAE.
Legal Issues
November 2002
ASHRAE Journal
67
as all inclusive. For example, many states do not require engi-
neering teachers to be licensed. Also, many courts do not re-
quire expert witnesses to be licensed in the state in which the
court is located. If you are asked to testify as an expert witness
and licensure is not required, discuss with your client and your
client’s attorney whether licensure is desirable.
If licensure is necessary, obtain the appropriate forms and pa-
perwork from the state engineering board. State requirements for
licensing by comity or endorsement are typically quite consis-
tent. Most require basic personal information, information about
educational background (including degrees and transcripts), work
history and initial licensure, plus as many as five professional
reference letters, and payment of a fee. If your state’s require-
ments for licensure at the time you were licensed were not at least
equivalent to the current requirements in the state where licen-
sure is desired, you may have to submit additional documenta-
tion regarding your work history and/or take examinations.
Some states permit engineers to obtain licensure on a tempo-
rary basis, typically for a fixed period of time or for the duration
of a single project. Often, temporary licensure is quicker and
easier to obtain. Temporary licensure also saves fees.
If you plan to seek licensure in multiple states and you want
to avoid the need to assemble educational information, tran-
scripts, original licensure information, and letters of reference
each time you apply, consider registering with the National
Council of Examiners for Engineering and Surveying
(“NCEES”), which will set up and maintain a file containing
these items and forward them to the appropriate licensing au-
thorities when requested to do so. NCEES imposes set-up, re-
newal and transmittal fees. Registration with NCEES does not
guarantee favorable action on applications.
In addition to licensing requirements for the engineer, many
states also require the engineer’s firm or corporation to obtain
a “certificate of authority,” which authorizes the firm to render
professional engineering services in the state. A certificate of
authority typically requires disclosure of the name of the firm
or corporation, and a designation by the firm’s chief executive
of an engineer who is licensed to practice in the state who will
represent the firm. Some states may also require the firm to
register with the Secretary of State. Contact the engineering
board in the subject state for appropriate application forms
and specific requirements.
In summary, if an opportunity for work in a new state pre-
sents itself, be aware that you may have to address some pro-
fessional licensing issues. Most state engineering boards have
operational web sites where you can obtain governing regula-
tions and necessary forms. You can typically obtain additional
information from, or address specific questions to, the contact
persons identified on the Web site.
The information contained in this article represents the opinion of
the author. It is not intended to, and does not, constitute legal advice,
nor does it represent the opinion of ASHRAE or any of its bodies.

ASHRAE Journal www.ashraejournal.org
